title: Jennifer Lopez

infobox.value: Jennifer Lynn Lopez   (1969-07-24) July 24, 1969 (age 56)  [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), U.S.
* Singer * songwriter[[3]](#cite_note-5) * actress * dancer * producer * businesswoman

* [Discography](/wiki/Jennifer_Lopez_discography "Jennifer Lopez discography") * [songs recorded](/wiki/List_of_songs_recorded_by_Jennifer_Lopez "List of songs recorded by Jennifer Lopez") * [videography](/wiki/Jennifer_Lopez_videography "Jennifer Lopez videography") * [filmography](/wiki/Jennifer_Lopez_filmography "Jennifer Lopez filmography") * [performances](/wiki/List_of_Jennifer_Lopez_live_performances "List of Jennifer Lopez live performances")

* Ojani Noa  ​  ​ (m. 1997; div. 1998)​ * Cris Judd  ​  ​ (m. 2001; div. 2003)​ * [Marc Anthony](/wiki/Marc_Anthony "Marc Anthony")  ​  ​ (m. 2004; div. 2014)​ * [Ben Affleck](/wiki/Ben_Affleck "Ben Affleck")  ​  ​ (m. 2022; div. 2025)​
[Full list](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Jennifer_Lopez "List of awards and nominations received by Jennifer Lopez")

* [Pop](/wiki/Pop_music "Pop music") * [R&B](/wiki/Contemporary_R%26B "Contemporary R&B") * [Latin](/wiki/Latin_music "Latin music") * [hip hop](/wiki/Hip_hop "Hip hop") * [dance](/wiki/Dance_music "Dance music")

* [Work](/wiki/Work_Group "Work Group") * [Epic](/wiki/Epic_Records "Epic Records") * [Columbia](/wiki/Columbia_Records "Columbia Records") * [Island](/wiki/Island_Records "Island Records") * [Capitol](/wiki/Capitol_Records "Capitol Records") * Hitco * [BMG](/wiki/BMG_Rights_Management "BMG Rights Management")

(born July 24, 1969)[[4]](#cite_note-6) is an American singer, songwriter, actress, dancer and businesswoman. Lopez is regarded as one of the most influential entertainers of her time, credited with breaking barriers for [Latino Americans](/wiki/Latino_Americans "Latino Americans") in [Hollywood](/wiki/Cinema_of_the_United_States "Cinema of the United States") and helping propel the [Latin pop movement](/wiki/Latin_American_music_in_the_United_States#1990s_Latin_Explosion "Latin American music in the United States") in music. She is also known for her [cultural impact](/wiki/Cultural_impact_of_Jennifer_Lopez "Cultural impact of Jennifer Lopez") through fashion, [branding](/wiki/Celebrity_branding "Celebrity branding"), and shifting [mainstream beauty standards](/wiki/Feminine_beauty_ideal "Feminine beauty ideal").

**Jennifer Lynn Lopez** (born July 24, 1969)[[4]](#cite_note-6) is an American singer, songwriter, actress, dancer and businesswoman. Lopez is regarded as one of the most influential entertainers of her time, credited with breaking barriers for [Latino Americans](/wiki/Latino_Americans "Latino Americans") in [Hollywood](/wiki/Cinema_of_the_United_States "Cinema of the United States") and helping propel the [Latin pop movement](/wiki/Latin_American_music_in_the_United_States#1990s_Latin_Explosion "Latin American music in the United States") in music. She is also known for her [cultural impact](/wiki/Cultural_impact_of_Jennifer_Lopez "Cultural impact of Jennifer Lopez") through fashion, [branding](/wiki/Celebrity_branding "Celebrity branding"), and shifting [mainstream beauty standards](/wiki/Feminine_beauty_ideal "Feminine beauty ideal").

After appearing as a [Fly Girl](/wiki/List_of_In_Living_Color_cast_members#Fly_Girls_(house_dance_troupe) "List of In Living Color cast members") dancer on the [sketch comedy](/wiki/Sketch_comedy "Sketch comedy") series *[In Living Color](/wiki/In_Living_Color "In Living Color")* (1991–1993), Lopez rose to wider fame as an actress with leading film roles in *[Selena](/wiki/Selena_(film) "Selena (film)")* (1997), *[Anaconda](/wiki/Anaconda_(1997_film) "Anaconda (1997 film)")* (1997), *[Out of Sight](/wiki/Out_of_Sight "Out of Sight")* (1998), and *[The Cell](/wiki/The_Cell_(film) "The Cell (film)")* (2000). She successfully ventured into music with her debut album, *[On the 6](/wiki/On_the_6 "On the 6")* (1999), which spawned the US [*Billboard* Hot 100](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100") number-one single "[If You Had My Love](/wiki/If_You_Had_My_Love "If You Had My Love")". With her second album *[J.Lo](/wiki/J.Lo_(album) "J.Lo (album)")* and the [romantic comedy](/wiki/Romantic_comedy "Romantic comedy") *[The Wedding Planner](/wiki/The_Wedding_Planner "The Wedding Planner")* (both 2001), she became the first woman to simultaneously have the number-one album and film in the United States. Her musical success continued with *[J to tha L–O! The Remixes](/wiki/J_to_tha_L%E2%80%93O!_The_Remixes "J to tha L–O! The Remixes")*—the first remix album to top the US [*Billboard* 200](/wiki/Billboard_200 "Billboard 200")—and *[This Is Me... Then](/wiki/This_Is_Me..._Then "This Is Me... Then")* (both 2002). These projects included the US number-one singles "[I'm Real](/wiki/I%27m_Real "I'm Real")", "[Ain't It Funny (Murder Remix)](/wiki/Ain%27t_It_Funny_(Murder_Remix) "Ain't It Funny (Murder Remix)")", and "[All I Have](/wiki/All_I_Have_(song) "All I Have (song)")".

Lopez established herself as one of Hollywood's highest-paid actresses with her roles in romantic comedies, including *[Maid in Manhattan](/wiki/Maid_in_Manhattan "Maid in Manhattan")* (2002), *[Shall We Dance?](/wiki/Shall_We_Dance%3F_(2004_film) "Shall We Dance? (2004 film)")* (2004), and *[Monster-in-Law](/wiki/Monster-in-Law "Monster-in-Law")* (2005). She also continued to make music, including *[Como Ama una Mujer](/wiki/Como_Ama_una_Mujer "Como Ama una Mujer")* (2007)―which yielded the highest first-week sales in the US of any debut Spanish album―and *[Love?](/wiki/Love%3F "Love?")* (2011), which spawned the best-selling single of her career, "[On the Floor](/wiki/On_the_Floor "On the Floor")". Following her role in the [police drama](/wiki/Police_drama "Police drama") series *[Shades of Blue](/wiki/Shades_of_Blue_(TV_series) "Shades of Blue (TV series)")* (2016–2018), Lopez's film career saw an upturn with her critically acclaimed performance in the crime drama *[Hustlers](/wiki/Hustlers_(film) "Hustlers (film)")* (2019). She has since starred in *[Marry Me](/wiki/Marry_Me_(2022_film) "Marry Me (2022 film)")* (2022), *[The Mother](/wiki/The_Mother_(2023_film) "The Mother (2023 film)")* (2023), *[This Is Me... Now: A Love Story](/wiki/This_Is_Me..._Now:_A_Love_Story "This Is Me... Now: A Love Story")* and *[Atlas](/wiki/Atlas_(2024_film) "Atlas (2024 film)")* (both 2024), and received praise for her supporting roles in *[Unstoppable](/wiki/Unstoppable_(2024_film) "Unstoppable (2024 film)")* (2024) and *[Kiss of the Spider Woman](/wiki/Kiss_of_the_Spider_Woman_(2025_film) "Kiss of the Spider Woman (2025 film)")* (2025).

Lopez has sold over 80 million records worldwide, while her films have cumulatively grossed over US$1.9 billion. Her [accolades](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Jennifer_Lopez "List of awards and nominations received by Jennifer Lopez") include a star on the [Hollywood Walk of Fame](/wiki/Hollywood_Walk_of_Fame "Hollywood Walk of Fame"), the [*Billboard* Icon Award](/wiki/Billboard_Icon_Award "Billboard Icon Award"), three [American Music Awards](/wiki/American_Music_Awards "American Music Awards"), four [MTV Video Music Awards](/wiki/MTV_Video_Music_Awards "MTV Video Music Awards") (including the [Michael Jackson Video Vanguard Award](/wiki/Michael_Jackson_Video_Vanguard_Award "Michael Jackson Video Vanguard Award")), and six *[Guinness World Records](/wiki/Guinness_World_Records "Guinness World Records")*. She has been ranked among the [100 most influential people in the world](/wiki/Time_100 "Time 100") by *[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")* (2018) and the [World's 100 Most Powerful Women](/wiki/Forbes_list_of_the_World%27s_100_Most_Powerful_Women "Forbes list of the World's 100 Most Powerful Women") by *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")* (2012). Lopez has a large social media following, being one of the [most-followed individuals](/wiki/List_of_most-followed_Instagram_accounts "List of most-followed Instagram accounts") on [Instagram](/wiki/Instagram "Instagram"). Outside of music and acting, Lopez served as a judge on *[American Idol](/wiki/American_Idol "American Idol")* (2011–2016) and *[World of Dance](/wiki/World_of_Dance_(TV_series) "World of Dance (TV series)")* (2017–2020). Her other ventures include a [lifestyle brand](/wiki/J.Lo_by_Jennifer_Lopez "J.Lo by Jennifer Lopez"), beauty and fashion lines, fragrances, a [production company](/wiki/Nuyorican_Productions "Nuyorican Productions"), and a [charitable foundation](/wiki/Lopez_Family_Foundation "Lopez Family Foundation").

## Early life

Jennifer Lynn Lopez was born on July 24, 1969, in [the Bronx](/wiki/The_Bronx "The Bronx"), one of the [boroughs of New York City](/wiki/Boroughs_of_New_York_City "Boroughs of New York City"), and raised in its [Castle Hill](/wiki/Castle_Hill,_Bronx "Castle Hill, Bronx") neighborhood.[[5]](#cite_note-7)[[6]](#cite_note-Gallick2003-8)[[7]](#cite_note-biography.com-9) Her parents, Guadalupe Rodríguez and David Lopez, were born in [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Puerto_Rico "Puerto Rico") and met in New York City.[[8]](#cite_note-Cartlidge-book-10)[[9]](#cite_note-11) After serving in the Army, David worked as a computer technician at [Guardian Insurance Company](/wiki/The_Guardian_Life_Insurance_Company_of_America "The Guardian Life Insurance Company of America").[[10]](#cite_note-12) Guadalupe was a homemaker for the first ten years of Lopez's life, later working as a [Tupperware](/wiki/Tupperware "Tupperware") salesperson[[11]](#cite_note-HuffPost-13) and a kindergarten and gym teacher.[[12]](#cite_note-14) They divorced in the 1990s after 33 years of marriage.[[11]](#cite_note-HuffPost-13)

Lopez is a middle child; she has an older sister, Leslie, and a younger sister, [Lynda](/wiki/Lynda_Lopez "Lynda Lopez").[[13]](#cite_note-15) The three shared a bed.[[14]](#cite_note-16)[[15]](#cite_note-wmagazine-17) Lopez has described her upbringing as "strict".[[16]](#cite_note-robinson-18) She was raised in a [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic") family; she attended [Mass](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy) "Mass (liturgy)") every Sunday and received a [Catholic education](/wiki/Catholic_school "Catholic school"), attending Holy Family School and the all-girls [Preston High School](/wiki/Preston_High_School_(New_York_City) "Preston High School (New York City)"), a [private school](/wiki/Private_school "Private school").[[17]](#cite_note-actorsstudio-19) In school, Lopez [ran track](/wiki/Ran_track "Ran track") on a national level, participated in gymnastics, and was on the softball team.[[18]](#cite_note-rantrack-20) She danced in school musicals and played a lead role in a production of *[Godspell](/wiki/Godspell "Godspell")*.[[19]](#cite_note-Parish_21-21) She described herself as a "tomboy" and "very athletic".[[20]](#cite_note-22)

There was "lots of music" in the typically Puerto Rican household,[[21]](#cite_note-23) and Lopez and her sisters were encouraged to sing, dance, and create their own plays for family events.[[22]](#cite_note-behind-24)[[23]](#cite_note-atriplethreat-25) *[West Side Story](/wiki/West_Side_Story "West Side Story")* made a particular impression on the young Lopez, who wanted to be an entertainer from an early age.[[17]](#cite_note-actorsstudio-19) As a teenager, Lopez became "obsessive" about dance, stating that "I practiced until my legs and feet ached".[[24]](#cite_note-26) She learned flamenco, jazz, and ballet at the [Kips Bay Boys & Girls Club](/wiki/Boys_%26_Girls_Clubs_of_America "Boys & Girls Clubs of America")[[17]](#cite_note-actorsstudio-19) and taught dance to younger students, including [Kerry Washington](/wiki/Kerry_Washington "Kerry Washington").[[25]](#cite_note-27) After graduating from high school, she had a part-time secretarial job at a law firm and studied business at New York's [Baruch College](/wiki/Baruch_College "Baruch College") for one semester.[[26]](#cite_note-28)[[27]](#cite_note-29) At age 18, she enrolled as a full-time student at Manhattan's Phil Black Dance Studio, where she had already taken night classes in jazz and tap dance.[[16]](#cite_note-robinson-18)[[15]](#cite_note-wmagazine-17)[[17]](#cite_note-actorsstudio-19) Her parents were unhappy with her decision to leave college to pursue a dance career. According to Lopez, her parents felt it was "foolish" because "no Latinas did that".[[19]](#cite_note-Parish_21-21) Her mother asked her to move out of the family home, and they stopped speaking for eight months.[[22]](#cite_note-behind-24)[[28]](#cite_note-30) Lopez moved to Manhattan, sleeping in the dance studio's office for the first few months.[[16]](#cite_note-robinson-18)[[15]](#cite_note-wmagazine-17)[[17]](#cite_note-actorsstudio-19)

## Career

### 1989–1996: Professional dancing and early acting roles

Lopez's first professional job came in 1989 when she spent five months touring Europe with the musical revue show *Golden Musicals of Broadway*. She was upset at being the only member of the [chorus](/wiki/Ensemble_(musical_theatre) "Ensemble (musical theatre)") not to have a solo, and later characterized it as a pivotal moment where she had to "try harder and become that much more committed".[[29]](#cite_note-31)[[30]](#cite_note-LA_Times_99-32) In 1990, she danced alongside [MC Hammer](/wiki/MC_Hammer "MC Hammer") in an episode of *[Yo! MTV Raps](/wiki/Yo!_MTV_Raps "Yo! MTV Raps")*,[[31]](#cite_note-33) and traveled around Japan for four months as a chorus member in *Synchronicity*.[[17]](#cite_note-actorsstudio-19) When she returned to the United States, she was hired as a backup dancer for [New Kids on the Block](/wiki/New_Kids_on_the_Block "New Kids on the Block")'s performance of "[Games](/wiki/Games_(New_Kids_on_the_Block_song) "Games (New Kids on the Block song)")" at the [1991 American Music Awards](/wiki/1991_American_Music_Awards "1991 American Music Awards").[[32]](#cite_note-34) She also traveled around America with regional productions of the musicals *[Jesus Christ Superstar](/wiki/Jesus_Christ_Superstar "Jesus Christ Superstar")* and *[Oklahoma!](/wiki/Oklahoma! "Oklahoma!")*.[[33]](#cite_note-35) During this period, Lopez also danced in music videos including [Doug E. Fresh](/wiki/Doug_E._Fresh "Doug E. Fresh")'s "Summertime", Richard Rogers' "Can't Stop Loving You", [EPMD](/wiki/EPMD "EPMD")'s "[Rampage](/wiki/Rampage_(song) "Rampage (song)")",[[34]](#cite_note-36) and [Samantha Fox](/wiki/Samantha_Fox "Samantha Fox")'s "[(Hurt Me! Hurt Me!) But the Pants Stay On](/wiki/(Hurt_Me!_Hurt_Me!)_But_the_Pants_Stay_On "(Hurt Me! Hurt Me!) But the Pants Stay On")".[[35]](#cite_note-37) Lopez stated: "I'd dance in a piece-of-garbage rap or pop video for 50 bucks and make the money last a whole month."[[36]](#cite_note-38)

Her most high-profile job as a professional dancer was as a [Fly Girl](/wiki/List_of_In_Living_Color_cast_members#Fly_Girls_(house_dance_troupe) "List of In Living Color cast members") [jazz-funk dancer](/wiki/Jazz-funk_dance "Jazz-funk dance") on the sketch comedy television series *[In Living Color](/wiki/In_Living_Color "In Living Color")*. The show's choreographer, [Rosie Perez](/wiki/Rosie_Perez "Rosie Perez") said she chose Lopez because "she had that look that I knew the audience would tune in to".[[37]](#cite_note-Parish27-39) Lopez moved to Los Angeles in late 1991 for the job; she filmed *In Living Color* during the day and attended acting classes taught by Aaron Speiser at night.[[17]](#cite_note-actorsstudio-19) Lopez felt ostracized by the other Fly Girls because of her "voluptuous figure", and also clashed with Perez.[[37]](#cite_note-Parish27-39) The head of [Virgin Records](/wiki/Virgin_Records "Virgin Records") considered signing The Fly Girls as a girl group to rival the [Spice Girls](/wiki/Spice_Girls "Spice Girls"), but the deal fell apart.[[30]](#cite_note-LA_Times_99-32) After appearing as a Fly Girl in seasons three and four of *In Living Color*, Lopez left to work as a backup dancer for [Janet Jackson](/wiki/Janet_Jackson "Janet Jackson"), and appeared in the music video for "[That's the Way Love Goes](/wiki/That%27s_the_Way_Love_Goes_(Janet_Jackson_song) "That's the Way Love Goes (Janet Jackson song)")".[[30]](#cite_note-LA_Times_99-32) She was scheduled to tour with Jackson on her [Janet World Tour](/wiki/Janet_World_Tour "Janet World Tour") in late 1993 but opted to pursue an acting career instead.[[38]](#cite_note-40) Music industry executives expressed interest in giving Lopez a record deal, but she chose to focus on acting.[[39]](#cite_note-Lazic-41) She hired *In Living Color* producer Eric Gold as her manager. He advised Lopez to lose weight if she wanted to succeed as an actress.[[40]](#cite_note-42)

Lopez's first professional acting job was a small recurring role on the television show *[South Central](/wiki/South_Central_(TV_series) "South Central (TV series)")* (1994). She was invited to audition for the pilot by a casting director who had seen her speak to camera during a behind-the-scenes *In Living Color* segment.[[17]](#cite_note-actorsstudio-19) She then acquired an agent and was cast in the [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS") show *[Second Chances](/wiki/Second_Chances_(American_TV_series) "Second Chances (American TV series)")*; the series was quickly cancelled, but her popularity with viewers led to her being cast in its spin-off *[Hotel Malibu](/wiki/Hotel_Malibu "Hotel Malibu")*.[[41]](#cite_note-43) She subsequently appeared in the television film *[Lost in the Wild](/wiki/Lost_in_the_Wild "Lost in the Wild")* (1993).[[42]](#cite_note-44) For her first major movie role, in [Gregory Nava](/wiki/Gregory_Nava "Gregory Nava")'s 1995 drama *[Mi Familia](/wiki/Mi_Familia "Mi Familia")*, Lopez received a nomination for the [Independent Spirit Award for Best Supporting Actress](/wiki/Independent_Spirit_Award_for_Best_Supporting_Actress "Independent Spirit Award for Best Supporting Actress").[[43]](#cite_note-45) She then starred in the action comedy *[Money Train](/wiki/Money_Train "Money Train")* (1995). The film was not a box office success, though her performance was reviewed positively,[[44]](#cite_note-Marquez_1-46) which led to her being further noticed in Hollywood.[[45]](#cite_note-47) In 1996, Lopez had a supporting role opposite [Robin Williams](/wiki/Robin_Williams "Robin Williams") in the comedy *[Jack](/wiki/Jack_(1996_film) "Jack (1996 film)")*, which director [Francis Ford Coppola](/wiki/Francis_Ford_Coppola "Francis Ford Coppola") cast her in after seeing her performance in *Mi Familia*.[[44]](#cite_note-Marquez_1-46) She next starred opposite [Jack Nicholson](/wiki/Jack_Nicholson "Jack Nicholson") as a "calculating Cuban maid" in the neo-noir thriller *[Blood and Wine](/wiki/Blood_and_Wine "Blood and Wine")* (1996),[[39]](#cite_note-Lazic-41) where David Rooney of *Variety* felt she delivered in "juggling" the "smoldering and soulful sides" of the character.[[46]](#cite_note-48)

### 1997–1999: Breakthrough with *Selena* and *On the 6*

With her casting as the singer [Selena Quintanilla-Pérez](/wiki/Selena_Quintanilla-P%C3%A9rez "Selena Quintanilla-Pérez") in the biopic *[Selena](/wiki/Selena_(film) "Selena (film)")* (1997), Lopez became the first Latina actress to earn $1 million. She described her salary for the film as a "statement to the world",[[47]](#cite_note-NominatedOscarSelena-49) but expressed disappointment that other Latina actors were not being afforded the same opportunities.[[48]](#cite_note-50) Despite having previously worked with the film's director Gregory Nava on *Mi Familia*, Lopez participated in an intense auditioning process and spent time with the late singer's family in [Corpus Christi, Texas](/wiki/Corpus_Christi,_Texas "Corpus Christi, Texas") before filming began.[[49]](#cite_note-51)[[50]](#cite_note-52) *Selena* was a box office hit, and Lopez's performance received critical acclaim.[[51]](#cite_note-53)[[52]](#cite_note-54) [Roger Ebert](/wiki/Roger_Ebert "Roger Ebert") of the *Chicago Sun-Times* described it as "a star-making performance" and wrote: "She has the star presence to look convincing in front of 100,000 fans."[[53]](#cite_note-55) Lopez received her first [Golden Globe](/wiki/55th_Golden_Globe_Awards "55th Golden Globe Awards") nomination for the performance. Nava asked the heads of Warner Bros. to fund an Academy Award campaign for Lopez but was told the academy would "never nominate a Latina."[[54]](#cite_note-56) Later in 1997, Lopez starred opposite [Ice Cube](/wiki/Ice_Cube "Ice Cube") in the horror film *[Anaconda](/wiki/Anaconda_(1997_film) "Anaconda (1997 film)")*, which received negative reviews from critics but was a box office success.[[55]](#cite_note-57) [Joe Leydon](/wiki/Joe_Leydon "Joe Leydon") of *Variety* found the film "silly" but said it deserved "a little credit" for being "the first movie of its kind to have a Latina and an African-American" as its stars.[[56]](#cite_note-58) In the crime film *[U Turn](/wiki/U_Turn_(1997_film) "U Turn (1997 film)")* (1997),[[57]](#cite_note-59) Lopez appeared topless in a sex scene that was added by director [Oliver Stone](/wiki/Oliver_Stone "Oliver Stone") during filming. Speaking in 2003, Lopez said it was not something "I would have chosen to do" and that she and Stone fought over it: "It's hard being the only woman on a set."[[17]](#cite_note-actorsstudio-19)

Lopez starred opposite [George Clooney](/wiki/George_Clooney "George Clooney") in the crime caper *[Out of Sight](/wiki/Out_of_Sight "Out of Sight")* (1998), [Steven Soderbergh](/wiki/Steven_Soderbergh "Steven Soderbergh")'s adaptation of the [Elmore Leonard](/wiki/Elmore_Leonard "Elmore Leonard") [1996 novel](/wiki/Out_of_Sight_(novel) "Out of Sight (novel)").[[58]](#cite_note-60) Cast as a deputy federal marshal who falls for a charming criminal, she won rave reviews for her performance.[[59]](#cite_note-Parish65-61)[[60]](#cite_note-Kyle-62) [Janet Maslin](/wiki/Janet_Maslin "Janet Maslin") of *The New York Times* described it as her "best movie role thus far, and she brings it both seductiveness and grit; if it was hard to imagine a hard-working, pistol-packing bombshell on the page, it couldn't be easier here."[[61]](#cite_note-63) Turan of the *Los Angeles Times* described Lopez as "an actress who can be convincingly tough and devastatingly erotic" and said the film solidified "her position as a woman you can confidently build a film around."[[62]](#cite_note-64) In 2021, Peter Bradshaw of *The Guardian* named Lopez and Clooney's partnership as one of the best examples of on-screen chemistry in cinema history.[[63]](#cite_note-65) Also in 1998, Lopez provided the voice of Azteca in the animated film *[Antz](/wiki/Antz "Antz")*.[[59]](#cite_note-Parish65-61)

Lopez decided to pursue a music career after working on *Selena*, realizing that she had missed "the excitement of the stage".[[30]](#cite_note-LA_Times_99-32) Her new manager [Benny Medina](/wiki/Benny_Medina "Benny Medina") sought to position her as "a brand name that will cross over into all media."[[30]](#cite_note-LA_Times_99-32) Lopez, who was "really feeling [her] Latin roots",[[64]](#cite_note-66) recorded a Spanish-language demo for circulation among prospective labels. With Lopez being a high-profile personality, a bidding war ensued.[[65]](#cite_note-Bruns_299-67) [Tommy Mottola](/wiki/Tommy_Mottola "Tommy Mottola"), the head of [Sony Music](/wiki/Sony_Music "Sony Music")'s [Work Group](/wiki/Work_Group "Work Group"), signed her but suggested that she sing in English instead.[[66]](#cite_note-68) Her record deal with Sony was described as "lucrative", having outbid other offers from [Capitol Records](/wiki/Capitol_Records "Capitol Records") and [EMI Latin](/wiki/EMI_Latin "EMI Latin").[[67]](#cite_note-69) While Lopez worked on her debut album, Sony "began a major push" to feature her with other high-profile entertainers, leading her to appear in the [Sean Combs](/wiki/Sean_Combs "Sean Combs") music video "[Been Around the World](/wiki/Been_Around_the_World "Been Around the World")" (1997).[[65]](#cite_note-Bruns_299-67)

Her debut album, *[On the 6](/wiki/On_the_6 "On the 6")* (1999), named after the [6 Subway line](/wiki/6_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "6 (New York City Subway service)") which connected her childhood home in the Bronx to Manhattan,[[68]](#cite_note-70) was met with positive reviews and further propelled her public profile.[[69]](#cite_note-Parish2-71)[[70]](#cite_note-Recording-Academy-72) The album was a success and Lopez's debut single, "[If You Had My Love](/wiki/If_You_Had_My_Love "If You Had My Love")", topped the U.S. [*Billboard* Hot 100](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100") for five consecutive weeks,[[70]](#cite_note-Recording-Academy-72) with another single, "[Waiting for Tonight](/wiki/Waiting_for_Tonight "Waiting for Tonight")", reaching number eight and becoming a celebratory anthem for the new millennium.[[71]](#cite_note-Shemesh-73) Other singles from the album included "[Let's Get Loud](/wiki/Let%27s_Get_Loud "Let's Get Loud")", which became one of Lopez's signature songs, and "[No Me Ames](/wiki/No_Me_Ames "No Me Ames")", a duet with future husband [Marc Anthony](/wiki/Marc_Anthony "Marc Anthony").[[70]](#cite_note-Recording-Academy-72) In July 1999, she performed "If You Had My Love" and "Let's Get Loud" at the [1999 FIFA Women's World Cup](/wiki/1999_FIFA_Women%27s_World_Cup "1999 FIFA Women's World Cup") closing ceremony. It was watched by over one billion viewers worldwide, exposing Lopez's music career to a larger international audience.[[72]](#cite_note-74)

### 2000–2002: Box office success, *J.Lo* and *This Is Me... Then*

In February 2000, Lopez and then-boyfriend [Sean Combs](/wiki/Sean_Combs "Sean Combs") attended the [Grammy Awards](/wiki/42nd_Annual_Grammy_Awards "42nd Annual Grammy Awards"), with Lopez wearing a plunging [green Versace silk chiffon dress](/wiki/Green_Versace_dress_of_Jennifer_Lopez "Green Versace dress of Jennifer Lopez").[[73]](#cite_note-PhillipsReay2002-75) The dress generated worldwide attention and became the most popular search query in Google's history, leading to the creation of [Google Images](/wiki/Google_Images "Google Images"),[[74]](#cite_note-Google_Images-76) while boosting Lopez's album and movie ticket sales.[[75]](#cite_note-Lee2003-77) Later that year, Lopez starred in the psychological thriller *[The Cell](/wiki/The_Cell_(film) "The Cell (film)")*, for which she received a $4 million salary.[[69]](#cite_note-Parish2-71) The film, where Lopez played a psychologist who uses radical experimental therapy to enter the mind of a killer, received mixed reviews but was a box office success.[[76]](#cite_note-78) David Edelstein of *Slate* remarked that the "imperious" Lopez was "trying to look waifishly expectant"[[77]](#cite_note-79) while Amy Taubin of *The Village Voice* noted that she appeared to be engaged "in some kind of pouting competition" "in lieu of acting."[[78]](#cite_note-80)

In January 2001, Lopez became the first woman to have a number-one film and album simultaneously in the United States, with the release of her second album, *[J.Lo](/wiki/J.Lo_(album) "J.Lo (album)")*, and her first romantic comedy *[The Wedding Planner](/wiki/The_Wedding_Planner "The Wedding Planner")*, which co-starred [Matthew McConaughey](/wiki/Matthew_McConaughey "Matthew McConaughey").[[79]](#cite_note-timeTyrangiel-81) Lopez had been searching for a romantic comedy role for several years; she wanted to show that she could be "every girl", stating: "I felt like all the women in romantic comedies always looked the same way, they were always white."[[80]](#cite_note-Wong-82) Despite negative reviews, the film opened at number one at the box office and solidified her place in Hollywood.[[81]](#cite_note-83)[[82]](#cite_note-Vice-84)[[80]](#cite_note-Wong-82) At the same time, *J.Lo* debuted at number one on the US [*Billboard* 200](/wiki/Billboard_200 "Billboard 200"); she was the first female solo recording artist under [Epic Records](/wiki/Epic_Records "Epic Records") to do so since the label's inception in 1953.[[83]](#cite_note-85) *J.Lo* was titled after the nickname she had been given by her fans, with Lopez also adopting J.Lo as her stagename.[[84]](#cite_note-Divas-86) The album received mixed critical reviews but became the most successful of her career, selling 12 million copies worldwide.[[85]](#cite_note-87)[[86]](#cite_note-88) It produced four singles: "[Love Don't Cost a Thing](/wiki/Love_Don%27t_Cost_a_Thing_(song) "Love Don't Cost a Thing (song)")", which reached number one in various countries,[[70]](#cite_note-Recording-Academy-72) "[Play](/wiki/Play_(Jennifer_Lopez_song) "Play (Jennifer Lopez song)")", "[I'm Real](/wiki/I%27m_Real "I'm Real")", and "[Ain't It Funny](/wiki/Ain%27t_It_Funny "Ain't It Funny")". A remix of "I'm Real" featuring rapper [Ja Rule](/wiki/Ja_Rule "Ja Rule") propelled that song to number one on the *Billboard* Hot 100.[[87]](#cite_note-I'm_Real_ET-89)

In September 2001, Lopez performed a [two-concert residency](/wiki/Let%27s_Get_Loud_(concerts) "Let's Get Loud (concerts)") in Puerto Rico, which marked her first concerts. It was broadcast that November as an [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC") special, *Jennifer Lopez in Concert*, and became one of the most-watched concert specials of the year, with a US audience of almost 12 million.[[88]](#cite_note-90) Also in 2001, Lopez founded her production company, [Nuyorican Productions](/wiki/Nuyorican_Productions "Nuyorican Productions"), with manager Benny Medina, and starred in the romantic drama, *[Angel Eyes](/wiki/Angel_Eyes_(film) "Angel Eyes (film)")*, opposite [Jim Caviezel](/wiki/Jim_Caviezel "Jim Caviezel").[[89]](#cite_note-91) Lopez's performance was well-reviewed,[[90]](#cite_note-92)[[91]](#cite_note-93) though the film was a critical and commercial disappointment.[[92]](#cite_note-Woog_55-94) She was slated to star alongside [Ricky Martin](/wiki/Ricky_Martin "Ricky Martin") in a remake of [Elvis Presley](/wiki/Elvis_Presley "Elvis Presley")'s *[Viva Las Vegas](/wiki/Viva_Las_Vegas "Viva Las Vegas")*, but this did not eventuate.[[93]](#cite_note-95)

> There was a time when I was very overworked and I was doing music and movies and so many things. I was suffering from a lack of sleep ... I was like — I don't want to move, I don't want to talk, I don't want to do anything. It was on that movie, *[Enough](/wiki/Enough_(film) "Enough (film)")* ... I had a [nervous breakdown](/wiki/Nervous_breakdown "Nervous breakdown").

—Lopez, discussing the "height of her fame"[[94]](#cite_note-Singh-96)

Lopez released two albums in 2002. The first was *[J to tha L–O! The Remixes](/wiki/J_to_tha_L%E2%80%93O!_The_Remixes "J to tha L–O! The Remixes")*, which became the first remix album in history to debut at number one on the *Billboard* 200.[[95]](#cite_note-Billboard_Book_of_Number_One_Hits-97) Its singles included "[Ain't It Funny (Murder Remix)](/wiki/Ain%27t_It_Funny_(Murder_Remix) "Ain't It Funny (Murder Remix)")" and "[I'm Gonna Be Alright](/wiki/I%27m_Gonna_Be_Alright "I'm Gonna Be Alright")", which reached number one and ten on the *Billboard* Hot 100, respectively.[[96]](#cite_note-Biggest_Billboard-98) Lopez's third studio album, *[This Is Me... Then](/wiki/This_Is_Me..._Then "This Is Me... Then")*, was released in late 2002, and was heavily influenced by her relationship with then-fiancé [Ben Affleck](/wiki/Ben_Affleck "Ben Affleck"). It received mixed critical reviews; *Billboard*'s Michael Paoletta noted the "considerable growth she reveals as a performer and tunesmith" on the album,[[97]](#cite_note-Paoletta-99) while [Jon Caramanica](/wiki/Jon_Caramanica "Jon Caramanica") called it "the least interesting music on the pop charts today."[[98]](#cite_note-100) The album became a commercial success, achieving the highest opening sales week of her career. The album's lead single, "[Jenny from the Block](/wiki/Jenny_from_the_Block "Jenny from the Block")", which peaked at number three on the *Billboard* Hot 100, was viewed as a response to the notion that Lopez had alienated her "core fan base: the black and Hispanic hip-hop community."[[99]](#cite_note-Lynette-101) The album also spawned the US number-one single "[All I Have](/wiki/All_I_Have_(song) "All I Have (song)")".[[70]](#cite_note-Recording-Academy-72)

Lopez starred as an abused wife who fights back in the thriller *[Enough](/wiki/Enough_(film) "Enough (film)")* (2002), a role which required her to learn [Krav Maga](/wiki/Krav_Maga "Krav Maga").[[92]](#cite_note-Woog_55-94)[[100]](#cite_note-102) An overworked Lopez suffered a [nervous breakdown](/wiki/Nervous_breakdown "Nervous breakdown") in 2001 while filming it.[[94]](#cite_note-Singh-96) The film received negative reviews;[[92]](#cite_note-Woog_55-94) Ebert of the *[Chicago Sun-Times](/wiki/Chicago_Sun-Times "Chicago Sun-Times")* felt it was "tacky material" and was surprised to see "an actress like Jennifer Lopez" involved with the project.[[101]](#cite_note-103) Her next film was the romantic comedy, *[Maid in Manhattan](/wiki/Maid_in_Manhattan "Maid in Manhattan")* (2002), in which Lopez starred opposite [Ralph Fiennes](/wiki/Ralph_Fiennes "Ralph Fiennes") as a maid and single mother from the Bronx.[[102]](#cite_note-104) It became the highest-grossing film of her career at the time.[[103]](#cite_note-BOM_Breakdown-105) Reviewing *Maid in Manhattan*, A. O. Scott of *The New York Times* enjoyed Lopez as a romantic lead and said "her greatest skill as an actress" was "her ability to melt without cracking the hard shell of composure she wears."[[104]](#cite_note-106) Lopez was ranked among the top ten highest-paid actresses in Hollywood in 2002, earning $8 million per feature.[[105]](#cite_note-107)

### 2003–2006: Media scrutiny

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/JenniferLopez.jpg/250px-JenniferLopez.jpg)](/wiki/File:JenniferLopez.jpg)

Lopez arriving at the [MTV Video Music Awards](/wiki/2004_MTV_Video_Music_Awards "2004 MTV Video Music Awards") in 2004

Lopez became the subject of widespread media criticism in 2003 due to her public relationship with Ben Affleck and the [tabloid depiction of her](#Public_image) as a demanding diva.[[106]](#cite_note-Observer-108) She fired both her personal manager Benny Medina and her publicist in mid-2003; *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* reported that movie executives had become frustrated by having their communications with Lopez "largely filtered" through Medina.[[107]](#cite_note-109) That year, Lopez starred opposite Affleck in the romantic comedy *[Gigli](/wiki/Gigli "Gigli")*, which was a [box-office bomb](/wiki/Box-office_bomb "Box-office bomb") and is considered [one of the worst films of all time](/wiki/List_of_films_considered_the_worst "List of films considered the worst").[[103]](#cite_note-BOM_Breakdown-105)[[108]](#cite_note-110)[[109]](#cite_note-111) Rex Reed of *[The Observer](/wiki/The_Observer "The Observer")* criticized the lead actors, calling them "pathetically incompetent" in both "craft and talent".[[110]](#cite_note-112) [Roger Ebert](/wiki/Roger_Ebert "Roger Ebert") agreed that the movie "doesn't quite work", but found Lopez and Affleck "appealing in their performances" and noted the couple's chemistry.[[111]](#cite_note-113)

Lopez had a minor role opposite Affleck in the film *[Jersey Girl](/wiki/Jersey_Girl_(2004_film) "Jersey Girl (2004 film)")* (2004). Following test audiences' negative reactions to the onscreen couple, her screen time was halved.[[112]](#cite_note-114) While she remained one of the highest-paid actresses in Hollywood during this period,[[113]](#cite_note-115)[[114]](#cite_note-116) Lopez later described this as the lowest point of her career[[115]](#cite_note-117) and admitted she felt "eviscerated" by the media coverage surrounding *Gigli*: "I lost my sense of self, questioned if I belonged in this business, thought maybe I did suck at everything. And my relationship self-destructed in front of the entire world. It was a two-year thing for me until I picked myself up again."[[116]](#cite_note-BeautyAndRedemption-118) Months later, she starred opposite [Richard Gere](/wiki/Richard_Gere "Richard Gere") in the romantic comedy-drama *[Shall We Dance?](/wiki/Shall_We_Dance%3F_(2004_film) "Shall We Dance? (2004 film)")*, which was a box-office success.[[103]](#cite_note-BOM_Breakdown-105)

She released her fourth studio album, *[Rebirth](/wiki/Rebirth_(Jennifer_Lopez_album) "Rebirth (Jennifer Lopez album)")*, in early 2005. Its title was symbolic of Lopez's hopes for "a new professional beginning".[[117]](#cite_note-Tracy_107-119) It was recorded during a period where Lopez felt "a little bit lost, trying to get my footing in a new life", "I had just gotten married [to singer [Marc Anthony](/wiki/Marc_Anthony "Marc Anthony")] ... I wasn't with Benny [Medina]."[[118]](#cite_note-Van_Meter-120) In a review of *Rebirth*, journalist [Alexis Petridis](/wiki/Alexis_Petridis "Alexis Petridis") remarked that the title "suggests even Lopez has realised that something is amiss with her career ... Despite the highlights, you're still left pondering the question: what happened to Jennifer Lopez?"[[119]](#cite_note-121) The album reached number two on the *Billboard* 200, but failed to replicate the sales of her previous albums.[[120]](#cite_note-Variety-122) Its lead single, "[Get Right](/wiki/Get_Right "Get Right")", however, reached number twelve on the *Billboard* Hot 100, becoming one of her most successful songs.[[96]](#cite_note-Biggest_Billboard-98)

The marketing for the romantic comedy *[Monster-in-Law](/wiki/Monster-in-Law "Monster-in-Law")* (2005), in which Lopez starred opposite [Jane Fonda](/wiki/Jane_Fonda "Jane Fonda"), played up her "*Gigli*-and-tabloid tarnished image", and it became a box office success.[[103]](#cite_note-BOM_Breakdown-105)[[121]](#cite_note-123) She next starred alongside [Robert Redford](/wiki/Robert_Redford "Robert Redford") and [Morgan Freeman](/wiki/Morgan_Freeman "Morgan Freeman") in the drama *[An Unfinished Life](/wiki/An_Unfinished_Life "An Unfinished Life")* (2005), which received mixed reviews. Ebert of the *Chicago Sun-Times* predicted that the typical review would be unkind: "It will have no respect for Jennifer Lopez, because she is going through a period right now when nobody is satisfied with anything she does ... Give Lopez your permission to be good again; she is the same actress now as when we thought her so new and fine."[[122]](#cite_note-124)

The next year, she returned to the top five of the *Billboard* Hot 100 as a featured artist on "[Control Myself](/wiki/Control_Myself "Control Myself")", the lead single from [LL Cool J](/wiki/LL_Cool_J "LL Cool J")'s [twelfth studio album](/wiki/Todd_Smith_(album) "Todd Smith (album)").[[123]](#cite_note-125) In 2006, she reunited with director Gregory Nava to star in the crime drama *[Bordertown](/wiki/Bordertown_(2006_film) "Bordertown (2006 film)")* as a journalist investigating [female homicides in Ciudad Juárez](/wiki/Female_homicides_in_Ciudad_Ju%C3%A1rez "Female homicides in Ciudad Juárez"), Mexico.[[124]](#cite_note-126) The film was negatively reviewed and received a [direct-to-video](/wiki/Direct-to-video "Direct-to-video") release.[[103]](#cite_note-BOM_Breakdown-105) David D'Arcy of *Screen Daily* found Lopez "unconvincing" as a journalist.[[125]](#cite_note-127)

### 2007–2010: Career downturn

By the late 2000s, Lopez's commercial success had declined. Her music career "entered a plateau", while "her film career entered a period of some disappointing-to-middling successes".[[126]](#cite_note-Piatkowski-128) In 2007, she starred opposite her then-husband [Marc Anthony](/wiki/Marc_Anthony "Marc Anthony") in the music biopic *[El Cantante](/wiki/El_Cantante "El Cantante")*, which told the story of Puerto Rican [salsa](/wiki/Salsa_music "Salsa music") singer [Héctor Lavoe](/wiki/H%C3%A9ctor_Lavoe "Héctor Lavoe") and his wife Puchi.[[127]](#cite_note-CantanteNYReview-129) It did not perform well at the box office and received mixed reviews from film critics; Lopez's performance, which she expressed pride in,[[128]](#cite_note-Pitchfork-130) drew both praise and criticism.[[117]](#cite_note-Tracy_107-119)[[129]](#cite_note-131)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Jennifer_Lopez_2008.jpg/250px-Jennifer_Lopez_2008.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jennifer_Lopez_2008.jpg)

Lopez in 2008

Lopez released two studio albums in 2007. Her fifth album, *[Como Ama una Mujer](/wiki/Como_Ama_una_Mujer "Como Ama una Mujer")*, was her first to be recorded entirely in Spanish.[[130]](#cite_note-CAUM-132) Chris Willman of *[Entertainment Weekly](/wiki/Entertainment_Weekly "Entertainment Weekly")* acknowledged that the album offered "fairly persuasive proof" that Lopez can sing, but was unimpressed by the "flaccid torch songs."[[131]](#cite_note-133) It became the fifth Spanish album to debut in the top ten of the *Billboard* 200, and achieved the highest first-week sales for an artist's debut Spanish album at the time.[[132]](#cite_note-Billboard_Spanish_Album_2016-134) The lead single, "[Qué Hiciste](/wiki/Qu%C3%A9_Hiciste "Qué Hiciste")", reached number 86 on the *Billboard* Hot 100 while the second single, "[Me Haces Falta](/wiki/Me_Haces_Falta "Me Haces Falta")", failed to chart. Lopez's sixth studio album, *[Brave](/wiki/Brave_(Jennifer_Lopez_album) "Brave (Jennifer Lopez album)")*, released later that year, was her lowest-charting album worldwide.[[133]](#cite_note-lovereview-135) Jonathan Bernstein of *[Entertainment Weekly](/wiki/Entertainment_Weekly "Entertainment Weekly")* was disappointed that Lopez had returned to "listless vocals" for her "back-to-the-dance-floor album."[[134]](#cite_note-Bernstein-136) The album debuted at number 12 on the *Billboard* 200[[135]](#cite_note-137) and produced two singles, "[Do It Well](/wiki/Do_It_Well "Do It Well")" and "[Hold It Don't Drop It](/wiki/Hold_It_Don%27t_Drop_It "Hold It Don't Drop It")". The first peaked at number 31 on the [*Billboard* Hot 100](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100") chart, while the latter failed to chart. While pregnant with twins, Lopez embarked upon her first ever concert tour, [a show co-headlined by Anthony](/wiki/Jennifer_Lopez_and_Marc_Anthony_en_Concierto "Jennifer Lopez and Marc Anthony en Concierto"), in September 2007; the tour was well received.[[117]](#cite_note-Tracy_107-119)[[136]](#cite_note-138) She also created, produced and was featured in the MTV show *[DanceLife](/wiki/DanceLife "DanceLife")*, which she described as a "passion project".[[117]](#cite_note-Tracy_107-119)

After giving birth to twins in February 2008, Lopez took a career break.[[137]](#cite_note-Tampa-139) She described the next few years as a "strange time" as she primarily focused on family and travelling with Anthony while he toured.[[138]](#cite_note-140) After rehiring former manager Medina,[[139]](#cite_note-141) Lopez released two songs in late 2009, "[Louboutins](/wiki/Louboutins_(song) "Louboutins (song)")" and "Fresh Out of the Oven". The songs were intended for her seventh studio album but failed to make an appearance on the *Billboard* charts, leading to her departure from Sony Music and [Epic Records](/wiki/Epic_Records "Epic Records").[[140]](#cite_note-142) Lopez's first theatrical role in three years was in the romantic comedy *[The Back-up Plan](/wiki/The_Back-up_Plan "The Back-up Plan")* (2010).[[137]](#cite_note-Tampa-139) Manohla Dargis of *The New York Times* was unimpressed by the movie and described Lopez as "an appealing screen presence with a disappointing big-screen track record. That's probably not all her fault: romantic roles for women often are the provenance of the bland or the blonde."[[141]](#cite_note-143)

### 2011–2015: *American Idol* and career revival

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Jennifer_Lopez_12%2C_2012.jpg/250px-Jennifer_Lopez_12%2C_2012.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jennifer_Lopez_12,_2012.jpg)

Lopez performing during her [Dance Again World Tour](/wiki/Dance_Again_World_Tour "Dance Again World Tour") in [Paris, France](/wiki/Paris,_France "Paris, France") in 2012

A "big turning point" in Lopez's career came when she joined the judging panel of the singing competition series *[American Idol](/wiki/American_Idol "American Idol")* for its [tenth season](/wiki/American_Idol_season_10 "American Idol season 10"), replacing [Simon Cowell](/wiki/Simon_Cowell "Simon Cowell"). Despite being advised that it was a "huge gamble",[[142]](#cite_note-Stylist-144) she accepted the job, as she was "not getting offered a whole bunch of movies".[[143]](#cite_note-varietyidol-145) Lopez's appearance on *American Idol* in 2011 returned her to prominence.[[143]](#cite_note-varietyidol-145) Hannah Elliot of *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")* described it as "a remarkable comeback", writing: "*Idol* humanized her. Viewers who knew only an attention-grabbing siren met a hardworking, self-made, empathetic single mother, who got emotional when contestants did well and when they failed."[[144]](#cite_note-Forbes2-146)

Lopez's exposure on *American Idol* resulted in what *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")* called "the most impressive reality-TV-based rejuvenation of a music career ever."[[145]](#cite_note-Rejuvenation-147) After signing a new recording contract with [Island Records](/wiki/Island_Records "Island Records"), her seventh studio album, *[Love?](/wiki/Love%3F "Love?")*, was released in early 2011. While the album itself was a moderate commercial success,[[133]](#cite_note-lovereview-135) the single "[On the Floor](/wiki/On_the_Floor "On the Floor")" was the year's highest-selling single by a female artist,[[146]](#cite_note-Digital_report_2012-148) and ultimately became the best-selling single of her career.[[120]](#cite_note-Variety-122) It reached number three on the *Billboard* Hot 100, becoming her highest-charting song as a lead artist since "All I Have".[[147]](#cite_note-149) The album produced two more singles, "[I'm Into You](/wiki/I%27m_Into_You "I'm Into You")" and "[Papi](/wiki/Papi_(Jennifer_Lopez_song) "Papi (Jennifer Lopez song)")", which did not achieve similar success.[[145]](#cite_note-Rejuvenation-147) By the end of 2011, Lopez was deemed the "world's greatest musical comeback act", based on a study which analysed over one billion [Google](/wiki/Google "Google") searches since 2004.[[148]](#cite_note-150)

Lopez returned as a judge for *American Idol*'s [eleventh season](/wiki/American_Idol_season_11 "American Idol season 11") in 2012, earning a reported $20 million.[[144]](#cite_note-Forbes2-146) That year, she released her greatest hits album, *[Dance Again... the Hits](/wiki/Dance_Again..._the_Hits "Dance Again... the Hits")*, to fulfil her contractual obligations with her former label Epic Records.[[149]](#cite_note-151) Lopez, who was divorcing Anthony and navigating the "breakup of a family", felt as if the album's sole single, "[Dance Again](/wiki/Dance_Again "Dance Again")", had come to her at the "perfect moment".[[150]](#cite_note-ryaninterview-152) "Dance Again" reached number 17 on the U.S. *Billboard* Hot 100. Lopez launched the [Dance Again World Tour](/wiki/Dance_Again_World_Tour "Dance Again World Tour"), her first headlining concert tour, in mid-2012. It grossed over $1 million per show.[[151]](#cite_note-Forbes_2013-153)

Lopez returned to the big screen in 2012, starring alongside an [ensemble cast](/wiki/Ensemble_cast "Ensemble cast") in the film *[What to Expect When You're Expecting](/wiki/What_to_Expect_When_You%27re_Expecting_(film) "What to Expect When You're Expecting (film)")*, which is based on the 1984 book *[What to Expect When You're Expecting](/wiki/What_to_Expect_When_You%27re_Expecting "What to Expect When You're Expecting")*.[[152]](#cite_note-Lopez-154) Lopez voiced Shira, a saber tooth tiger, in the animated film, *[Ice Age: Continental Drift](/wiki/Ice_Age:_Continental_Drift "Ice Age: Continental Drift")*, the fourth film in the *[Ice Age](/wiki/Ice_Age_(franchise) "Ice Age (franchise)")* franchise.[[153]](#cite_note-Rovi_Ice_Age_4-155)[[154]](#cite_note-156) Also in 2012, a talent show, *[¡Q'Viva! The Chosen](/wiki/%C2%A1Q%27Viva!_The_Chosen "¡Q'Viva! The Chosen")* followed Lopez, Anthony, and director-choreographer [Jamie King](/wiki/Jamie_King "Jamie King") as they travelled across 21 countries in Latin America to find new talent for a [Las Vegas](/wiki/Las_Vegas "Las Vegas") show.[[155]](#cite_note-157) In 2013, Lopez starred alongside [Jason Statham](/wiki/Jason_Statham "Jason Statham") in the crime thriller *[Parker](/wiki/Parker_(2013_film) "Parker (2013 film)")*, in which she played Leslie. Her performance earned positive reviews, with the *[Chicago Tribune](/wiki/Chicago_Tribune "Chicago Tribune")* commending the role for giving Lopez "an opportunity to be dramatic, romantic, funny, depressed, euphoric and violent. The audience stays with her all the way".[[156]](#cite_note-158) That year, Lopez released the single "[Live It Up](/wiki/Live_It_Up_(Jennifer_Lopez_song) "Live It Up (Jennifer Lopez song)")", and was named [chief creative officer](/wiki/Chief_creative_officer "Chief creative officer") of [NuvoTV](/wiki/NuvoTV "NuvoTV").[[157]](#cite_note-159)[[158]](#cite_note-Latino1-160) She also served as an executive producer of the television series *[The Fosters](/wiki/The_Fosters_(2013_TV_series) "The Fosters (2013 TV series)")* (2013–2018), a show about a lesbian couple raising a family. Lopez's desire to work on the series was driven by her late aunt, who was gay.[[159]](#cite_note-161)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Jennifer_Lopez_at_GLAAD_Media_Awards_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Jennifer_Lopez_at_GLAAD_Media_Awards_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jennifer_Lopez_at_GLAAD_Media_Awards_(cropped).jpg)

Lopez at the [25th GLAAD Media Awards](/wiki/25th_GLAAD_Media_Awards "25th GLAAD Media Awards") in 2014

After a one-season absence, Lopez returned to *American Idol* for its [thirteenth season](/wiki/American_Idol_season_13 "American Idol season 13"), earning a reported $17.5 million.[[160]](#cite_note-162) Her eighth studio album, *[A.K.A.](/wiki/A.K.A._(album) "A.K.A. (album)")*, was released in mid-2014 through [Capitol Records](/wiki/Capitol_Records "Capitol Records"), experiencing lacklustre sales, becoming her lowest-selling album in the U.S.[[161]](#cite_note-163) The album produced three singles: "[I Luh Ya Papi](/wiki/I_Luh_Ya_Papi "I Luh Ya Papi")", featuring [French Montana](/wiki/French_Montana "French Montana"), "[First Love](/wiki/First_Love_(Jennifer_Lopez_song) "First Love (Jennifer Lopez song)")", and "[Booty](/wiki/Booty_(Jennifer_Lopez_song) "Booty (Jennifer Lopez song)")", featuring [Iggy Azalea](/wiki/Iggy_Azalea "Iggy Azalea"). They reached 77, 87 and 18 respectively on the *Billboard* Hot 100. Also that year, Lopez released "[We Are One (Ole Ola)](/wiki/We_Are_One_(Ole_Ola) "We Are One (Ole Ola)")", the official song for the [2014 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2014_FIFA_World_Cup "2014 FIFA World Cup") along with [Pitbull](/wiki/Pitbull_(rapper) "Pitbull (rapper)") and [Claudia Leitte](/wiki/Claudia_Leitte "Claudia Leitte"). Lopez released a book, *[True Love](/wiki/True_Love_(book) "True Love (book)")*, which became a [*New York Times* best-seller](/wiki/The_New_York_Times_Best_Seller_list "The New York Times Best Seller list").[[162]](#cite_note-164)

2015 saw the release of *[The Boy Next Door](/wiki/The_Boy_Next_Door_(film) "The Boy Next Door (film)")*, an erotic thriller that Lopez both co-produced and starred in as a high school teacher who becomes involved with a student, which eventually leads to his dangerous obsession with her.[[163]](#cite_note-HuffPost_Latino-165) The film received negative reviews from critics.[[164]](#cite_note-166) Despite this, it became her most successful opening at the box office for a live action film since *Monster-in-Law*.[[165]](#cite_note-167)[[166]](#cite_note-Box_Office_Mojo-168) Lopez had a voice role in the animated feature *[Home](/wiki/Home_(2015_comedy_film) "Home (2015 comedy film)")* and contributed the single "[Feel the Light](/wiki/Feel_the_Light "Feel the Light")" to the film's official soundtrack.[[167]](#cite_note-Martin_&_Lopez-169)[[168]](#cite_note-170) She also starred in the independent drama film *[Lila & Eve](/wiki/Lila_%26_Eve "Lila & Eve")*, alongside [Viola Davis](/wiki/Viola_Davis "Viola Davis").[[169]](#cite_note-171)

### 2016–2021: Television work, *Hustlers* and Super Bowl LIV

In January 2016, Lopez commenced a concert residency show, [All I Have](/wiki/All_I_Have_(residency) "All I Have (residency)"), at [Planet Hollywood](/wiki/Planet_Hollywood_Resort_%26_Casino "Planet Hollywood Resort & Casino")'s [Zappos Theater](/wiki/Zappos_Theater "Zappos Theater") in [Las Vegas](/wiki/Las_Vegas "Las Vegas").[[170]](#cite_note-172) She performed 120 shows during the three-year run, grossing over $100 million in ticket sales.[[171]](#cite_note-173)[[172]](#cite_note-174) At the beginning of the residency, Lopez signed a multi-album deal with her former label Epic Records[[173]](#cite_note-New_Epic_Deal-175)—though she would depart the label before releasing any albums—and released "[Ain't Your Mama](/wiki/Ain%27t_Your_Mama "Ain't Your Mama")", one of her most successful singles during the 2010s.[[174]](#cite_note-176) Also in 2016, she began starring in [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC")'s crime drama series *[Shades of Blue](/wiki/Shades_of_Blue_(TV_series) "Shades of Blue (TV series)")*, which she also executive produced. She played [Harlee Santos](/wiki/Harlee_Santos "Harlee Santos"), a single mother and police detective who goes undercover for the FBI to investigate her own squad.[[175]](#cite_note-177) The series' premiere brought NBC its most-watched Thursday debut in seven years.[[176]](#cite_note-178) Starring alongside [Ray Liotta](/wiki/Ray_Liotta "Ray Liotta"), Lopez's performance received critical praise.[[177]](#cite_note-179)[[178]](#cite_note-180) That year, she reprised her voice role as Shira in the animated film *[Ice Age: Collision Course](/wiki/Ice_Age:_Collision_Course "Ice Age: Collision Course")* (2016).[[179]](#cite_note-181)

Lopez was executive producer and judge on NBC's dance competition series *[World of Dance](/wiki/World_of_Dance_(TV_series) "World of Dance (TV series)")*,[[180]](#cite_note-Variety_July_2016-182) which was a ratings success upon its May 2017 premiere.[[181]](#cite_note-183)[[182]](#cite_note-184) That year, Lopez released the singles "[Ni Tú Ni Yo](/wiki/Ni_T%C3%BA_Ni_Yo "Ni Tú Ni Yo")" and "[Amor, Amor, Amor](/wiki/Amor,_Amor,_Amor "Amor, Amor, Amor")", which were intended to be included on her second Spanish-language album, *Por Primera Vez*,[[132]](#cite_note-Billboard_Spanish_Album_2016-134)[[183]](#cite_note-Idolator_1-185) which was ultimately shelved.

In 2018, Lopez released a string of Spanish and [Spanglish](/wiki/Spanglish "Spanglish") singles: "[Se Acabó el Amor](/wiki/Se_Acab%C3%B3_el_Amor "Se Acabó el Amor")", "[El Anillo](/wiki/El_Anillo "El Anillo")", "[Dinero](/wiki/Dinero_(Jennifer_Lopez_song) "Dinero (Jennifer Lopez song)")" featuring [DJ Khaled](/wiki/DJ_Khaled "DJ Khaled") and [Cardi B](/wiki/Cardi_B "Cardi B"), and "[Te Guste](/wiki/Te_Guste "Te Guste")" with [Bad Bunny](/wiki/Bad_Bunny "Bad Bunny"). *Forbes* noted that the songs were unable to "completely connect on a crossover, mainstream level", but were more successful on US-based Latin charts, nearly all of them reaching number one on the *Billboard* [Latin Airplay](/wiki/Latin_Airplay "Latin Airplay") chart.[[184]](#cite_note-Benjamin-186) She later starred in and executive produced the comedy film *[Second Act](/wiki/Second_Act_(film) "Second Act (film)")* (2018), directed by [Peter Segal](/wiki/Peter_Segal "Peter Segal");[[185]](#cite_note-187) she also recorded the single "[Limitless](/wiki/Limitless_(Jennifer_Lopez_song) "Limitless (Jennifer Lopez song)")" for its soundtrack.[[186]](#cite_note-188) *Second Act* earned mixed reviews from critics,[[187]](#cite_note-189) but performed well at the box office, grossing over $72 million on a $16 million budget.[[188]](#cite_note-Childress-190)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Jennifer_Lopez_Live_during_Its_my_party_tour.jpg/250px-Jennifer_Lopez_Live_during_Its_my_party_tour.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jennifer_Lopez_Live_during_Its_my_party_tour.jpg)

Lopez performing during her [It's My Party](/wiki/It%27s_My_Party_(tour) "It's My Party (tour)") tour in July 2019

In the first half of 2019, Lopez embarked on an international concert tour, [It's My Party](/wiki/It%27s_My_Party_(tour) "It's My Party (tour)"), to celebrate her 50th birthday. The tour grossed an estimated $54.7 million from thirty-eight shows. *Variety* reported that most shows were sold out.[[189]](#cite_note-191) She also signed with new label [Hitco Entertainment](/wiki/Hitco "Hitco") and released the single "[Medicine](/wiki/Medicine_(Jennifer_Lopez_song) "Medicine (Jennifer Lopez song)")" featuring French Montana.[[190]](#cite_note-192) She also became executive producer of two television series, *[Good Trouble](/wiki/Good_Trouble_(TV_series) "Good Trouble (TV series)")* and *[Thanks a Million](/wiki/Thanks_a_Million_(TV_series) "Thanks a Million (TV series)")*.[[191]](#cite_note-193)

Lopez executive produced and starred in the crime drama film *[Hustlers](/wiki/Hustlers_(film) "Hustlers (film)")* (2019). Directed by [Lorene Scafaria](/wiki/Lorene_Scafaria "Lorene Scafaria"), the film is inspired by a true story, following a group of [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") [strippers](/wiki/Stripper "Stripper") who con wealthy male [Wall Street](/wiki/Wall_Street "Wall Street") clientele.[[192]](#cite_note-194) Lopez's portrayal of a veteran stripper in *Hustlers* garnered acclaim from critics, with some deeming it the best performance of her acting career.[[193]](#cite_note-195)[[194]](#cite_note-196)[[195]](#cite_note-197) The film also gave Lopez her highest opening weekend at the box office for a live action film, grossing $33.2 million.[[188]](#cite_note-Childress-190)[[196]](#cite_note-198) Her performance received nominations for Best Supporting Actress at the [Golden Globe Awards](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Awards "Golden Globe Awards"), [Screen Actors Guild Awards](/wiki/Screen_Actors_Guild_Award "Screen Actors Guild Award"), [Critics' Choice Movie Awards](/wiki/Critics%27_Choice_Movie_Awards "Critics' Choice Movie Awards") and [Independent Spirit Awards](/wiki/Independent_Spirit_Awards "Independent Spirit Awards").[[197]](#cite_note-GiftedActress-199) The success of *Hustlers* was regarded by various media outlets as a comeback as an actress for Lopez.[[198]](#cite_note-200)[[199]](#cite_note-201)[[200]](#cite_note-202) Her perceived Academy Award snub for *Hustlers* (2019) was referenced by Los Angeles mayor [Eric Garcetti](/wiki/Eric_Garcetti "Eric Garcetti") when announcing a new initiative for Latino representation in Hollywood.[[201]](#cite_note-203)

In February 2020, Lopez co-headlined the [Super Bowl LIV halftime show](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LIV_halftime_show "Super Bowl LIV halftime show") in [Miami, Florida](/wiki/Miami,_Florida "Miami, Florida") alongside [Shakira](/wiki/Shakira "Shakira"); the performance included an appearance by her child Emme Muñiz.[[202]](#cite_note-204) The performance was widely praised and became the most-watched [Super Bowl halftime show](/wiki/Super_Bowl_halftime_show "Super Bowl halftime show") to date.[[203]](#cite_note-BBC-205) Later that year, Lopez released the singles "[Pa' Ti](/wiki/Pa%27_Ti "Pa' Ti")" with [Maluma](/wiki/Maluma "Maluma")―which became her most successful song since 2017―and "[In the Morning](/wiki/In_the_Morning_(Jennifer_Lopez_song) "In the Morning (Jennifer Lopez song)")", before headlining the 2021 *[Dick Clark's New Year's Rockin' Eve with Ryan Seacrest](/wiki/Dick_Clark%27s_New_Year%27s_Rockin%27_Eve_with_Ryan_Seacrest "Dick Clark's New Year's Rockin' Eve with Ryan Seacrest")* special live from [Times Square](/wiki/Times_Square "Times Square") on [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company").[[204]](#cite_note-206)[[205]](#cite_note-207)

In January 2021, she performed at the [2021 inauguration](/wiki/Inauguration_of_Joe_Biden "Inauguration of Joe Biden") of President [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden") in Washington, D.C.[[206]](#cite_note-Inauguration-208) In mid-2021, she signed a multi-year deal with [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix") to produce a range of films and television shows through her own Nuyorican Productions,[[207]](#cite_note-209) and released the [reggaeton](/wiki/Reggaeton "Reggaeton") Spanish single "[Cambia el Paso](/wiki/Cambia_el_Paso "Cambia el Paso")" with [Rauw Alejandro](/wiki/Rauw_Alejandro "Rauw Alejandro").[[128]](#cite_note-Pitchfork-130)

### 2022–present: Focus on film and *This Is Me... Now*

In January 2022, Lopez appeared as a guest on the [fourteenth season](/wiki/RuPaul%27s_Drag_Race_season_14 "RuPaul's Drag Race season 14") of *[RuPaul's Drag Race](/wiki/RuPaul%27s_Drag_Race "RuPaul's Drag Race")* in the episode titled "[She's a Super Tease](/wiki/She%27s_a_Super_Tease "She's a Super Tease")". She co-produced and starred opposite [Owen Wilson](/wiki/Owen_Wilson "Owen Wilson") and Maluma in the romantic comedy *[Marry Me](/wiki/Marry_Me_(2022_film) "Marry Me (2022 film)")* (2022).[[208]](#cite_note-210) The film grossed over $50 million at the box office while becoming the most-streamed day-and-date film on [Peacock](/wiki/Peacock_(streaming_service) "Peacock (streaming service)"),[[209]](#cite_note-211)[[210]](#cite_note-212) and received generally mixed reviews from critics.[[211]](#cite_note-213)[[212]](#cite_note-214) Lopez and Maluma released a [soundtrack for the film](/wiki/Marry_Me_(soundtrack) "Marry Me (soundtrack)"), which generated the singles "[On My Way](/wiki/On_My_Way_(Jennifer_Lopez_song) "On My Way (Jennifer Lopez song)")" and "[Marry Me](/wiki/Marry_Me_(Jennifer_Lopez_and_Maluma_song) "Marry Me (Jennifer Lopez and Maluma song)")". Her next project was the documentary *[Jennifer Lopez: Halftime](/wiki/Jennifer_Lopez:_Halftime "Jennifer Lopez: Halftime")* (2022), which focuses on her life following the release of *Hustlers* and in preparation for her Super Bowl performance.[[213]](#cite_note-215) Released on Netflix following its premiere at the [Tribeca Film Festival](/wiki/Tribeca_Film_Festival "Tribeca Film Festival"), it garnered positive reviews from film critics.[[214]](#cite_note-216)[[215]](#cite_note-217) The following month, [Jimmy Fallon](/wiki/Jimmy_Fallon "Jimmy Fallon") and Lopez released a children's book, *Con Pollo: A Bilingual Playtime Adventure*, which became a [*New York Times* best-seller](/wiki/The_New_York_Times_Best_Seller_list "The New York Times Best Seller list").[[216]](#cite_note-218)[[217]](#cite_note-219)

Lopez co-produced and starred opposite [Josh Duhamel](/wiki/Josh_Duhamel "Josh Duhamel") and [Jennifer Coolidge](/wiki/Jennifer_Coolidge "Jennifer Coolidge") in the action-comedy *[Shotgun Wedding](/wiki/Shotgun_Wedding_(2022_film) "Shotgun Wedding (2022 film)")* (2023). It became one of the top-streamed films on [Amazon Prime Video](/wiki/Amazon_Prime_Video "Amazon Prime Video") that year.[[218]](#cite_note-Variety_2024-220) She also led and co-produced the action thriller film *[The Mother](/wiki/The_Mother_(2023_film) "The Mother (2023 film)")* (2023).[[219]](#cite_note-221) The film received mixed reviews,[[220]](#cite_note-222) but became the most-watched film on [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix") in 2023,[[218]](#cite_note-Variety_2024-220)[[221]](#cite_note-223) and at one point, was one of the [most-watched original films of all time on the platform](/wiki/List_of_most-watched_Netflix_original_programming "List of most-watched Netflix original programming").[[222]](#cite_note-224)

After marrying [Ben Affleck](/wiki/Ben_Affleck "Ben Affleck") and entering a publishing partnership with [BMG Rights Management](/wiki/BMG_Rights_Management "BMG Rights Management"),[[223]](#cite_note-bmg_partnership-225) Lopez released her ninth studio album, *[This Is Me... Now](/wiki/This_Is_Me..._Now "This Is Me... Now")*, in February 2024.[[224]](#cite_note-226) A sequel to *This Is Me... Then* (2002), the album spawned two singles: "[Can't Get Enough](/wiki/Can%27t_Get_Enough_(Jennifer_Lopez_song) "Can't Get Enough (Jennifer Lopez song)")", and "Rebound" featuring rapper [Anuel AA](/wiki/Anuel_AA "Anuel AA").[[225]](#cite_note-227)[[226]](#cite_note-228) Lopez promoted the album with an exclusive concert at the [Orpheum Theatre](/wiki/Orpheum_Theatre_(Los_Angeles) "Orpheum Theatre (Los Angeles)") in Los Angeles, which was recorded as a concert film and released by [Apple TV+](/wiki/Apple_TV%2B "Apple TV+") on February 21, 2024.[[227]](#cite_note-229)

*This Is Me... Now* formed part of a "three-part multimedia project" which included a companion musical film, *[This Is Me... Now: A Love Story](/wiki/This_Is_Me..._Now:_A_Love_Story "This Is Me... Now: A Love Story")* directed by [Dave Meyers](/wiki/Dave_Meyers_(director) "Dave Meyers (director)"), and a documentary, *[The Greatest Love Story Never Told](/wiki/Greatest_Love_Story_Never_Told "Greatest Love Story Never Told")*.[[218]](#cite_note-Variety_2024-220)[[228]](#cite_note-230) Lopez plays a fictionalized version of herself in *A Love Story*, which was described as an "autobiographical musical rom-com action sci-fi"[[229]](#cite_note-231) inspired by her own "tumultuous dating history".[[230]](#cite_note-Lee-232) After potential partners backed out, Lopez financed the film herself for $20 million before it was purchased by Amazon.[[218]](#cite_note-Variety_2024-220) While *A Love Story* received positive reviews from critics and became a success on Prime Video,[[231]](#cite_note-D'Alessandro,_2024-233) the film and its accompanying documentary also divided audiences, due in part to the overexposure of her relationship with Affleck.[[230]](#cite_note-Lee-232)[[232]](#cite_note-Hassett-234) Lopez was set to embark on the [This Is Me... Live](/wiki/This_Is_Me..._Live "This Is Me... Live") North American tour, but cancelled it in order to spend more time with her family. Shortly after, it was announced that she and Affleck were divorcing.[[233]](#cite_note-235)[[234]](#cite_note-236) She would later state that "it was like my whole f\*\*\*ing world exploded" amid the public scrutiny surrounding her personal and professional life in 2024.[[232]](#cite_note-Hassett-234)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Katarina_Metzger_and_Jennifer_Lopez_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Katarina_Metzger_and_Jennifer_Lopez_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Katarina_Metzger_and_Jennifer_Lopez_(cropped).jpg)

Lopez in 2024

Despite facing public scrutiny throughout 2024, Lopez's films continued to achieve success; *[Collider](/wiki/Valnet "Valnet")* and *[Deadline Hollywood](/wiki/Deadline_Hollywood "Deadline Hollywood")* noted her to be among the most successful actors of the streaming era.[[230]](#cite_note-Lee-232)[[231]](#cite_note-D'Alessandro,_2024-233) She co-produced and starred in the [sci-fi](/wiki/Sci-fi "Sci-fi") thriller *[Atlas](/wiki/Atlas_(2024_film) "Atlas (2024 film)")* (2024), the third project under her Netflix deal.[[235]](#cite_note-237)[[236]](#cite_note-238) *Atlas* received negative reviews from critics but became a success on the platform.[[237]](#cite_note-239)[[238]](#cite_note-240) In a positive review of the film, *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* praised Lopez's performance and called the film "an intriguing concept."[[239]](#cite_note-241) Her next role was in the biographical sports drama *[Unstoppable](/wiki/Unstoppable_(2024_film) "Unstoppable (2024 film)")* (2024), in which she plays the mother of wrestler [Anthony Robles](/wiki/Anthony_Robles "Anthony Robles"). The film premiered at the [Toronto International Film Festival](/wiki/Toronto_International_Film_Festival "Toronto International Film Festival") and received limited theatrical release before debuting on Prime Video. Lopez received praise for her work in the film,[[240]](#cite_note-242) which Owen Gleiberman of *[Variety](/wiki/Variety_(magazine) "Variety (magazine)")* called "the fullest screen performance she has ever given".[[241]](#cite_note-243) She was presented with *Variety*'s "Legend & Groundbreaker Award" at the 2025 [Palm Springs International Film Festival](/wiki/Palm_Springs_International_Film_Festival "Palm Springs International Film Festival") in honor of *Unstoppable* and her overall career.[[242]](#cite_note-244)

Lopez returned to touring with [Up All Night: Live in 2025](/wiki/Up_All_Night:_Live_in_2025 "Up All Night: Live in 2025"), which consisted of twenty dates across Africa, Asia and Europe.[[243]](#cite_note-245) On July 24, 2025, Lopez's 56th birthday, she released the single "[Birthday](/wiki/Birthday_(Jennifer_Lopez_song) "Birthday (Jennifer Lopez song)")".[[244]](#cite_note-246) She co-executive produced and starred in the [Bill Condon](/wiki/Bill_Condon "Bill Condon")-directed musical drama *[Kiss of the Spider Woman](/wiki/Kiss_of_the_Spider_Woman_(2025_film) "Kiss of the Spider Woman (2025 film)")* (2025), a film adaption of the [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_theatre "Broadway theatre") [1993 musical of the same name](/wiki/Kiss_of_the_Spider_Woman_(musical) "Kiss of the Spider Woman (musical)"). Her first role in a full-fledged musical, Condon wrote the part with Lopez in mind.[[245]](#cite_note-Sasic-247) The film premiered at the [2025 Sundance Film Festival](/wiki/2025_Sundance_Film_Festival "2025 Sundance Film Festival"), where Lopez's performance received positive reviews.[[246]](#cite_note-248) In December 2025, she launched Jennifer Lopez: Up All Night Live in Las Vegas, a residency at [The Colosseum at Caesars Palace](/wiki/The_Colosseum_at_Caesars_Palace "The Colosseum at Caesars Palace") which will span 12 shows.[[247]](#cite_note-249) That month, she appeared in a [mid-credits scene](/wiki/Mid-credits_scene "Mid-credits scene") in *[Anaconda](/wiki/Anaconda_(2025_film) "Anaconda (2025 film)")* (2025), a meta-reboot of the 1997 film.[[248]](#cite_note-250)

On March 6, 2026, Lopez released the single "Save Me Tonight", in collaboration with French DJ/producer [David Guetta](/wiki/David_Guetta "David Guetta").[[249]](#cite_note-251)

#### Upcoming projects

Lopez will co-produce and star opposite [Brett Goldstein](/wiki/Brett_Goldstein "Brett Goldstein") in Netflix's *Office Romance*.[[250]](#cite_note-252) She will also co-produce a series adaptation of [Emily Henry](/wiki/Emily_Henry "Emily Henry")'s 2023 novel [*Happy Place*](/wiki/Happy_Place_(novel) "Happy Place (novel)") for Netflix.[[251]](#cite_note-253) She has also committed to lead and co-produce Netflix's film adaptation of the bestselling novel, *The Cipher*.[[252]](#cite_note-254) Lopez has several projects in the works as a producer.[[253]](#cite_note-255)[[254]](#cite_note-256)[[255]](#cite_note-257) Through her continued partnership with Netflix, she will also star in an adaption of the novel *[The Last Mrs. Parrish](/wiki/The_Last_Mrs._Parrish "The Last Mrs. Parrish")*, directed by [Robert Zemeckis](/wiki/Robert_Zemeckis "Robert Zemeckis").[[256]](#cite_note-258)

## Personal life

Lopez was in a nearly decade-long relationship with David Cruz, her high-school boyfriend, until the mid-1990s.[[257]](#cite_note-259) They were engaged at one point, and Cruz relocated to Los Angeles to be with her when she began pursuing her career.[[258]](#cite_note-260) She later said of Cruz, "You get lucky, you have a first love like that."[[259]](#cite_note-BrokeAllTheRules-261) She was married to Cuban waiter Ojani Noa from February 1997 to January 1998. In subsequent court cases, Noa was prevented from publishing a book about their marriage[[260]](#cite_note-262)[[261]](#cite_note-263) and from using private honeymoon footage of Lopez in a documentary.[[262]](#cite_note-264)[[263]](#cite_note-265)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Cris_Judd_on_RealTVfilms.jpg/250px-Cris_Judd_on_RealTVfilms.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cris_Judd_on_RealTVfilms.jpg)

Cris Judd in 2012

Lopez was in an [on-again, off-again relationship](/wiki/On-again,_off-again_relationship "On-again, off-again relationship") with record producer and rapper [Sean Combs](/wiki/Sean_Combs "Sean Combs") (then known as "Puff Daddy") from 1999 to early 2001.[[264]](#cite_note-266)[[265]](#cite_note-267) On the night of December 27, 1999, Lopez, Combs and rapper [Shyne](/wiki/Shyne "Shyne") were arrested and charged with criminal possession of a weapon and possession of stolen property, after leaving the scene of a shooting at a [Times Square](/wiki/Times_Square "Times Square") nightclub.[[266]](#cite_note-268) Charges against Lopez were dropped within an hour[[267]](#cite_note-269) while Combs was acquitted of all charges at trial in March 2001.[[268]](#cite_note-270) They broke up shortly thereafter.[[22]](#cite_note-behind-24) Shyne was sentenced to ten years in prison.[[269]](#cite_note-271) Lopez later said that, while she had "cared very much" about Combs, their "crazy, tumultuous" relationship "was always something I knew would end."[[270]](#cite_note-272)[[22]](#cite_note-behind-24) She was married to Cris Judd, her former backup dancer, from September 2001 to January 2003.[[271]](#cite_note-273)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Time_100_Jennifer_Lopez_and_Marc_Anthony_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Time_100_Jennifer_Lopez_and_Marc_Anthony_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Time_100_Jennifer_Lopez_and_Marc_Anthony_(cropped).jpg)

Lopez and [Marc Anthony](/wiki/Marc_Anthony "Marc Anthony"), 2006

Before her divorce with Judd was finalized, Lopez was in a relationship with actor and filmmaker [Ben Affleck](/wiki/Ben_Affleck "Ben Affleck") from mid-2002 to early 2004. Although they had crossed paths several times before (including at the [70th Academy Awards](/wiki/70th_Academy_Awards "70th Academy Awards") and at the premiere of *[Armageddon](/wiki/Armageddon_(1998_film) "Armageddon (1998 film)")*,[[272]](#cite_note-StyleRules-274) both in 1998), their first major meeting was on the set of *Gigli* (2003) in December 2001.[[273]](#cite_note-275)[[274]](#cite_note-276) They later worked together on the music video for "Jenny from the Block" and the film *Jersey Girl* (2004).[[275]](#cite_note-277)[[276]](#cite_note-278) Her album *This Is Me... Then* was dedicated to and inspired by Affleck. Their relationship was extensively publicized.[[277]](#cite_note-279) Tabloids referred to the couple as "[Bennifer](/wiki/Bennifer "Bennifer")", a portmanteau *Vanity Fair* described as "the first of that sort of tabloid branding".[[278]](#cite_note-280) They became engaged in November 2002,[[279]](#cite_note-281)[[280]](#cite_note-282) but their planned wedding on September 14, 2003, was postponed with four days' notice because of "excessive media attention".[[281]](#cite_note-283)[[282]](#cite_note-284) They ended their engagement in January 2004.[[283]](#cite_note-285) Years later, Lopez said Affleck's discomfort with media scrutiny was one reason for their split[[22]](#cite_note-behind-24)[[284]](#cite_note-286) and described it as her "first real heartbreak": "I think different time, different thing, who knows what could've happened, but there was a genuine love there."[[285]](#cite_note-287)

After her relationship with Affleck, Lopez stopped discussing her personal life in interviews.[[286]](#cite_note-288) She was married to singer [Marc Anthony](/wiki/Marc_Anthony "Marc Anthony") from June 2004 to June 2014;[[287]](#cite_note-289)[[288]](#cite_note-290) they had previously worked together and dated for a few months in the late 1990s.[[22]](#cite_note-behind-24)[[289]](#cite_note-291) Their wedding took place five months after the end of her relationship with Affleck. During their marriage, they collaborated on music and performed together, as well as co-starring in *[El Cantante](/wiki/El_Cantante "El Cantante")* (2006).[[290]](#cite_note-292) Lopez gave birth to fraternal twins, a boy and a girl, on [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island") in February 2008.[[291]](#cite_note-Welcomes_Twins-293)[[292]](#cite_note-294) *People* magazine paid a reported US$6 million for the first photographs of the twins, making them [the most expensive celebrity pictures ever taken](/wiki/List_of_most_expensive_celebrity_photographs "List of most expensive celebrity photographs") at the time.[[293]](#cite_note-295) In 2009, Anthony and Lopez purchased a stake in the [Miami Dolphins](/wiki/Miami_Dolphins "Miami Dolphins").[[294]](#cite_note-296) They announced their separation in July 2011. Anthony filed for divorce in April 2012[[295]](#cite_note-297) and it was finalized in June 2014. Lopez retained primary physical custody of the two children.[[2]](#cite_note-2014_name_change-3) Lopez occasionally performs with her daughter.[[296]](#cite_note-298)

Lopez had an on-again, off-again relationship with her former backup dancer Casper Smart from October 2011 to August 2016.[[297]](#cite_note-299)[[298]](#cite_note-300) She dated [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees") baseball player [Alex Rodriguez](/wiki/Alex_Rodriguez "Alex Rodriguez") from February 2017 to early 2021.[[299]](#cite_note-301)[[300]](#cite_note-302)[[116]](#cite_note-BeautyAndRedemption-118) They became engaged in March 2019[[301]](#cite_note-303) but postponed their wedding twice due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"). In response to tabloid speculation about the state of their relationship, they released a statement in March 2021, saying they were "working through some things".[[302]](#cite_note-304) They announced the end of their relationship in April 2021.[[303]](#cite_note-Today1-305)

In April 2021, Lopez and Affleck were reported to be dating again,[[304]](#cite_note-306) with Lopez publicly confirming their rekindled relationship that July.[[305]](#cite_note-307) In April 2022, Lopez announced their second engagement, 20 years after the first proposal.[[306]](#cite_note-308) They were married in Las Vegas on July 16, 2022,[[307]](#cite_note-309) and held a wedding celebration for family and friends the following month.[[308]](#cite_note-310) On August 20, 2024, Lopez filed for divorce from Affleck, citing April 26, 2024, as the date of their separation.[[309]](#cite_note-311) She also requested that her legal name be changed back to Jennifer Lynn Lopez.[[310]](#cite_note-Robinson-312) On January 6, 2025, the divorce was finalized.[[311]](#cite_note-313)

## Other activities

### Business ventures and endorsements

Lopez first ventured into product endorsement in 1998, becoming a national spokesperson for [Coca-Cola](/wiki/Coca-Cola "Coca-Cola") and [L'Oréal](/wiki/L%27Or%C3%A9al "L'Oréal").[[312]](#cite_note-314)[[313]](#cite_note-315) In 2001, Lopez launched her first business venture, the lifestyle brand [J.Lo by Jennifer Lopez](/wiki/J.Lo_by_Jennifer_Lopez "J.Lo by Jennifer Lopez").
