It included a clothing line which catered to women of all sizes, as Lopez felt "the voluptuous woman [was] almost ignored" in the fashion industry.[[84]](#cite_note-Divas-86) The following year, she opened Madre's, a Los Angeles restaurant serving Latin cuisine,[[314]](#cite_note-madre-316) and released her first fragrance, [Glow by JLo](/wiki/Glow_by_JLo "Glow by JLo"), through a partnership with [Coty, Inc.](/wiki/Coty,_Inc. "Coty, Inc."); it became the top-selling fragrance in the U.S.[[315]](#cite_note-HS-317) In 2003, she released a second fragrance, [Still Jennifer Lopez](/wiki/Still_Jennifer_Lopez "Still Jennifer Lopez"), and became the face of [Louis Vuitton](/wiki/Louis_Vuitton "Louis Vuitton")'s fall advertising campaign.[[316]](#cite_note-318) Lopez has since released 30 fragrances as of 2020.[[317]](#cite_note-319) Her clothing lines and two fragrances generated over $300 million in revenue throughout 2004.[[79]](#cite_note-timeTyrangiel-81)

In 2005, Lopez launched her second fashion label, Sweetface, which was geared towards "high fashion" in comparison to her previous fashion line.[[318]](#cite_note-320) She also appeared in a *[Kill Bill](/wiki/Kill_Bill "Kill Bill")*-inspired [Pepsi](/wiki/Pepsi "Pepsi") commercial with [Beyoncé](/wiki/Beyonc%C3%A9 "Beyoncé") and [David Beckham](/wiki/David_Beckham "David Beckham").[[319]](#cite_note-321) In the late 2000s, her restaurant Madre's closed permanently,[[314]](#cite_note-madre-316) as did her two fashion lines.[[144]](#cite_note-Forbes2-146) In 2011, in the midst of her career resurgence, Lopez became brand ambassador for various brands including [L'Oréal](/wiki/L%27Or%C3%A9al "L'Oréal"), [Gilette Venus](/wiki/Gillette_Mach3 "Gillette Mach3"), [Tous](/wiki/Tous_(company) "Tous (company)") and [Fiat](/wiki/Fiat "Fiat").[[144]](#cite_note-Forbes2-146) She also launched the [Jennifer Lopez Collection](/wiki/Jennifer_Lopez_Collection "Jennifer Lopez Collection"), a clothing and homeware line with [Tommy Hilfiger](/wiki/Tommy_Hilfiger "Tommy Hilfiger") for [Kohl's](/wiki/Kohl%27s "Kohl's"); the collection generated an estimated $3 billion in sales during its first year.[[142]](#cite_note-Stylist-144) The following year, she launched [Teeology](/wiki/Teeology "Teeology"), a luxury T-shirt brand.[[320]](#cite_note-322) In 2013, she founded the phone retail brand [Viva Móvil](/wiki/Viva_M%C3%B3vil "Viva Móvil"), which caters specifically to Latinos.[[321]](#cite_note-323)

In 2014, Lopez released a range of jewelry, designed in partnership with Endless Jewelry.[[322]](#cite_note-324) In 2016, she designed a capsule collection of shoes and jewelry in collaboration with [Giuseppe Zanotti](/wiki/Giuseppe_Zanotti "Giuseppe Zanotti").[[323]](#cite_note-325) The following year, she was announced as the new face of clothing company [Guess](/wiki/Guess_(company) "Guess (company)"), becoming the oldest spokeswoman in the company's history.[[324]](#cite_note-326) In collaboration with [Inglot Cosmetics](/wiki/Inglot_Cosmetics "Inglot Cosmetics"), Lopez launched a limited-edition makeup collection in 2018.[[325]](#cite_note-327) In 2019, she became the global face of the [Coach](/wiki/Coach_New_York "Coach New York") brand,[[326]](#cite_note-328) and launched a collection of sunglasses with the brand Quay Australia.[[327]](#cite_note-329) In 2020, after modelling an updated version of her iconic [Green Versace "jungle" dress](/wiki/Green_Versace_dress_of_Jennifer_Lopez "Green Versace dress of Jennifer Lopez") at [Milan Fashion Week](/wiki/Milan_Fashion_Week "Milan Fashion Week") the previous year, Lopez was the face of the brand's Spring/Summer 2020 campaign, which featured other pieces based on the same jungle print.[[328]](#cite_note-330)

Throughout 2021, Lopez focused on investments and creating her own brands. She launched her skin care company, JLo Beauty,[[329]](#cite_note-331) released a shoe collection in partnership with [DSW](/wiki/Designer_Shoe_Warehouse "Designer Shoe Warehouse"), and invested in companies including [Hims & Hers Health](/wiki/Hims_%26_Hers_Health "Hims & Hers Health"), [Bodyarmor SuperDrink](/wiki/Bodyarmor_SuperDrink "Bodyarmor SuperDrink"), Goli nutritional supplements and meal delivery service [Wonder](/wiki/Wonder_Group "Wonder Group").[[330]](#cite_note-332) In March 2022, she was appointed as the chief "entertainment and lifestyle" officer of the cruise line, [Virgin Voyages](/wiki/Virgin_Voyages "Virgin Voyages").[[331]](#cite_note-333) That September, she became the global ambassador of Italian lingerie label [Intimissimi](/wiki/Intimissimi "Intimissimi").[[332]](#cite_note-334) In 2023, Lopez launched a [spritz](/wiki/Spritz_(cocktail) "Spritz (cocktail)") brand named Delola in collaboration with mixologist [Lynnette Marrero](/wiki/Lynnette_Marrero "Lynnette Marrero").[[333]](#cite_note-335) That year, she partnered with fashion retailer Revolve to design a footwear line, JLO Jennifer Lopez, which was released as three collections in March, May, and June.[[334]](#cite_note-336)[[335]](#cite_note-337) In 2024, she released a lingerie collection with Intimissimi inspired by her album *This Is Me... Now*.[[336]](#cite_note-338)

### Philanthropy

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/P071014AL-0257_%2815156063116%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-P071014AL-0257_%2815156063116%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:P071014AL-0257_(15156063116)_(cropped).jpg)

Lopez and then First Lady [Michelle Obama](/wiki/Michelle_Obama "Michelle Obama") posing for a selfie at the [League of United Latin American Citizens](/wiki/League_of_United_Latin_American_Citizens "League of United Latin American Citizens") National Convention and Exposition in 2014[[337]](#cite_note-339)

Lopez was heavily involved in [charitable activities](/wiki/Charitable_organization "Charitable organization") following the [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks"). Joining other artists, she was featured on charitable singles such as "[What's Going On](/wiki/What%27s_Going_On_(remix_album) "What's Going On (remix album)")" and "[El Ultimo Adios (The Last Goodbye)](/wiki/El_Ultimo_Adios_(The_Last_Goodbye) "El Ultimo Adios (The Last Goodbye)")", which benefited people affected by the tragedy.[[338]](#cite_note-africa1-340)[[339]](#cite_note-artistlist-341) In February 2007, Lopez was honored with the Artists for Amnesty prize by [Amnesty International](/wiki/Amnesty_International "Amnesty International") for her work in *[Bordertown](/wiki/Bordertown_(2006_film) "Bordertown (2006 film)")*, which shed light on the hundreds of [feminicides in Ciudad Juárez](/wiki/Feminicides_in_Ciudad_Ju%C3%A1rez "Feminicides in Ciudad Juárez"). Lopez described it as "one of the world's most shocking and disturbing, underreported crimes against humanity".[[340]](#cite_note-342)[[341]](#cite_note-343) That year, part of the proceeds from Lopez and Anthony's [co-headlining North American concert tour](/wiki/Jennifer_Lopez_and_Marc_Anthony_en_Concierto "Jennifer Lopez and Marc Anthony en Concierto") was donated to the Run for Something Better program, which fights childhood obesity.[[342]](#cite_note-ld1-344)[[343]](#cite_note-345)

Lopez has been a long-time supporter of the [Children's Hospital Los Angeles](/wiki/Children%27s_Hospital_Los_Angeles "Children's Hospital Los Angeles"). Her album *[Rebirth](/wiki/Rebirth_(Jennifer_Lopez_album) "Rebirth (Jennifer Lopez album)")* (2005) is dedicated to Paige Peterson,[[344]](#cite_note-Album_Credits-346) an eleven-year-old cancer patient she befriended at the hospital who died in 2004. Lopez stated that "[she doesn't] like to do [her] charity work in public. That's not what you do it for."[[345]](#cite_note-Paige-347) In 2009, Lopez launched the [Lopez Family Foundation](/wiki/Lopez_Family_Foundation "Lopez Family Foundation")[[c]](#cite_note-348) alongside her sister, Lynda. The organization works to increase healthcare access for underprivileged women and children, including through a [telemedicine](/wiki/Telemedicine "Telemedicine") program in partnership with the Children's Hospital Los Angeles. The foundation has facilitated the expansion of medical facilities in [Panama](/wiki/Panama "Panama") and Puerto Rico, and created the Center for a Healthy Childhood at [Montefiore Medical Center](/wiki/Montefiore_Medical_Center "Montefiore Medical Center") in the Bronx.[[346]](#cite_note-349)[[347]](#cite_note-350)

In December 2012, Lopez held a charity drive to benefit her three favorite charities: the [Gloria Wise Boys and Girls Club](/wiki/Gloria_Wise_Boys_and_Girls_Club "Gloria Wise Boys and Girls Club"), the Children's Hospital Los Angeles and the [American Red Cross](/wiki/American_Red_Cross "American Red Cross"), mainly benefiting victims of [Hurricane Sandy](/wiki/Hurricane_Sandy "Hurricane Sandy").[[348]](#cite_note-HSp-351) In 2015, she became the first celebrity spokesperson for the [Children's Miracle Network Hospitals](/wiki/Children%27s_Miracle_Network_Hospitals "Children's Miracle Network Hospitals") and the [BC Children's Hospital Foundation](/wiki/BC_Children%27s_Hospital_Foundation "BC Children's Hospital Foundation") (BCCHF).[[349]](#cite_note-352) She also became the first Global Advocate for Girls and Women at the [United Nations Foundation](/wiki/United_Nations_Foundation "United Nations Foundation"), working on issues including maternal health care, education, and violence.[[350]](#cite_note-353)[[351]](#cite_note-354)

In September 2017, following [Hurricane Irma](/wiki/Hurricane_Irma "Hurricane Irma") and [Hurricane Maria](/wiki/Hurricane_Maria "Hurricane Maria"), Lopez donated $1 million from the proceeds of her Las Vegas residency to [humanitarian aid](/wiki/Humanitarian_aid "Humanitarian aid") for Puerto Rico.[[352]](#cite_note-355)[[353]](#cite_note-356) Along with [Marc Anthony](/wiki/Marc_Anthony "Marc Anthony"), she launched a humanitarian relief campaign entitled Somos Una Voz (English: We Are One Voice), an effort to rush supplies to areas affected by Hurricane Maria.[[354]](#cite_note-357) Lopez and Anthony presented a concert and telethon for disaster relief, "One Voice: Somos Live!", which raised over $35 million.[[355]](#cite_note-358) She was also among various artists featured on [Lin-Manuel Miranda](/wiki/Lin-Manuel_Miranda "Lin-Manuel Miranda")'s charity single "[Almost Like Praying](/wiki/Almost_Like_Praying "Almost Like Praying")", which benefits Puerto Rico.[[356]](#cite_note-359)

As of 2021, Lopez continues to regularly donate to and support charities.[[357]](#cite_note-360) In September 2021, she launched a philanthropic organization, Limitless Labs, which supports and empowers Latina entrepreneurs and business owners. Limitless Labs has entered partnerships with the [10,000 Small Businesses](/wiki/10,000_Small_Businesses "10,000 Small Businesses") initiative[[358]](#cite_note-361) and [Grameen America](/wiki/Grameen_America "Grameen America"), the latter being an effort to empower Latina businesses with $14 million in business loans and "6 million hours of financial education and training by 2030."[[359]](#cite_note-362)[[360]](#cite_note-363) Lopez headlined the [Los Angeles Dodgers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Dodgers "Los Angeles Dodgers") Foundation's Blue Diamond Gala in 2022, which raised $3.6 million for education, health care, homelessness and social justice causes.[[361]](#cite_note-364)

### Political views and activism

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/210120-D-WD757-1975_-_Jennifer_Lopez_at_the_US_Capitol_%2850860511978%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-210120-D-WD757-1975_-_Jennifer_Lopez_at_the_US_Capitol_%2850860511978%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:210120-D-WD757-1975_-_Jennifer_Lopez_at_the_US_Capitol_(50860511978)_(cropped).jpg)

Lopez performing at the [inauguration of Joe Biden](/wiki/Inauguration_of_Joe_Biden "Inauguration of Joe Biden") in 2021

A supporter of the [Democratic Party](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)"), Lopez has a long history of backing Democratic candidates for public office.[[362]](#cite_note-365) She endorsed President [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") in his [2012 presidential campaign](/wiki/Barack_Obama_presidential_campaign,_2012 "Barack Obama presidential campaign, 2012"), speaking in television advertisements and attending fundraising events for Obama.[[363]](#cite_note-366)[[364]](#cite_note-Bustle-367) She endorsed Democratic presidential nominee [Hillary Clinton](/wiki/Hillary_Clinton "Hillary Clinton") in 2016, headlining a free concert in Florida in support of her that October.[[364]](#cite_note-Bustle-367)[[365]](#cite_note-368)

Lopez has been critical of President [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump").[[366]](#cite_note-Halftime-369) During her [Super Bowl halftime show](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LIV_halftime_show "Super Bowl LIV halftime show") performance in 2020, she performed her song "[Let's Get Loud](/wiki/Let%27s_Get_Loud "Let's Get Loud")" while cloaked in a large [Puerto Rican flag](/wiki/Puerto_Rican_flag "Puerto Rican flag"), with children in metal cages displayed on the field. It was interpreted as a statement about the [Mexico–United States border crisis](/wiki/Mexico%E2%80%93United_States_border_crisis "Mexico–United States border crisis") and Trump's [immigration policy](/wiki/Immigration_policy_of_the_first_Donald_Trump_administration "Immigration policy of the first Donald Trump administration").[[206]](#cite_note-Inauguration-208)[[367]](#cite_note-Inauguration2-370) The [NFL](/wiki/NFL "NFL") attempted to remove the cages from the performance but Lopez refused.[[366]](#cite_note-Halftime-369) She endorsed President [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden") in his [2020 presidential campaign](/wiki/Joe_Biden_2020_presidential_campaign "Joe Biden 2020 presidential campaign").[[368]](#cite_note-371) She later performed at Biden's [2021 inauguration](/wiki/Inauguration_of_Joe_Biden "Inauguration of Joe Biden"), where she sang "[This Land Is Your Land](/wiki/This_Land_Is_Your_Land "This Land Is Your Land")" and "[America the Beautiful](/wiki/America_the_Beautiful "America the Beautiful")", while reciting the last phrase of the [Pledge of Allegiance](/wiki/Pledge_of_Allegiance "Pledge of Allegiance") in Spanish.[[206]](#cite_note-Inauguration-208) She also worked "Let's Get Loud" into the performance as a "reprise" to her political message at the Super Bowl.[[367]](#cite_note-Inauguration2-370) Lopez endorsed [Kamala Harris](/wiki/Kamala_Harris "Kamala Harris") during her [2024 presidential campaign](/wiki/Kamala_Harris_2024_presidential_campaign "Kamala Harris 2024 presidential campaign"). She spoke at a Las Vegas rally for Harris that November, pushing back on remarks made at Trump's [rally at Madison Square Garden](/wiki/2024_Trump_rally_at_Madison_Square_Garden "2024 Trump rally at Madison Square Garden") which were deemed offensive to Puerto Ricans.[[369]](#cite_note-372)[[370]](#cite_note-373)

Lopez is an avid supporter of [LGBTQ rights](/wiki/LGBTQ_rights "LGBTQ rights"), and has raised millions of dollars for [HIV/AIDS research](/wiki/HIV/AIDS_research "HIV/AIDS research").[[371]](#cite_note-amfAR-374) In June 2013, [amfAR](/wiki/AmfAR "AmfAR") presented her with its humanitarian award for her philanthropic work.[[371]](#cite_note-amfAR-374) That September, she was awarded the Ally for Equality award presented by the [Human Rights Campaign](/wiki/Human_Rights_Campaign "Human Rights Campaign"), for her support of the LGBTQ community.[[372]](#cite_note-375) The following year, she received the [GLAAD Vanguard Award](/wiki/GLAAD_Vanguard_Award "GLAAD Vanguard Award").[[373]](#cite_note-376) In July 2016, Lopez released a single entitled "[Love Make the World Go Round](/wiki/Love_Make_the_World_Go_Round "Love Make the World Go Round")", a collaboration with Lin-Manuel Miranda, which benefits victims of the [Orlando nightclub shooting](/wiki/Orlando_nightclub_shooting "Orlando nightclub shooting").[[374]](#cite_note-377) She was also featured on the song "[Hands](/wiki/Hands_(2016_song) "Hands (2016 song)")" along with numerous other artists, also benefiting those affected by the Orlando shooting.[[375]](#cite_note-378) Lopez signed an open letter from [*Billboard*](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)") magazine to the [United States Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") in 2016, which demanded action on [gun control](/wiki/Gun_control "Gun control").[[376]](#cite_note-379) In 2017, she participated in the #KeepThePromise campaign, initiated by the film *[The Promise](/wiki/The_Promise_(2016_film) "The Promise (2016 film)")*, a movie about the [Armenian genocide](/wiki/Armenian_genocide "Armenian genocide").[[377]](#cite_note-380)

In June 2020, Lopez attended a [Black Lives Matter](/wiki/Black_Lives_Matter "Black Lives Matter") movement protest in Los Angeles, in connection with the broader [George Floyd protests](/wiki/George_Floyd_protests "George Floyd protests").[[378]](#cite_note-381) Lopez has also been an active advocate for the [Time's Up movement](/wiki/Time%27s_Up_(organization) "Time's Up (organization)").[[379]](#cite_note-382) In January 2022, she became one of the co-chairs for [Michelle Obama](/wiki/Michelle_Obama "Michelle Obama")'s When We All Vote.[[380]](#cite_note-383) She expressed solidarity with the people of the [Gaza Strip](/wiki/Gaza_Strip "Gaza Strip") during the [Gaza war](/wiki/Gaza_war "Gaza war"). As part of a group called [Artists4Ceasefire](/wiki/Artists4Ceasefire "Artists4Ceasefire"), she signed a letter urging President Joe Biden to call for an immediate ceasefire in Gaza.[[381]](#cite_note-384)

## Artistry

### Influences

Lopez credited Puerto Rican actress [Rita Moreno](/wiki/Rita_Moreno "Rita Moreno")'s performance in the musical film *[West Side Story](/wiki/West_Side_Story_(1961_film) "West Side Story (1961 film)")* (1961) as the major influence on her career path and artistry.[[382]](#cite_note-385) Moreno was the only performer Lopez was able to identify with, at a time when Latinos were rarely on television.[[383]](#cite_note-386) She was also inspired by [Barbra Streisand](/wiki/Barbra_Streisand "Barbra Streisand")'s career as a singer, director and actress.[[197]](#cite_note-GiftedActress-199) Growing up, she was influenced by Latin music styles ranging from [salsa](/wiki/Salsa_(music) "Salsa (music)") to [bachata](/wiki/Bachata_(music) "Bachata (music)"), and artists including [Celia Cruz](/wiki/Celia_Cruz "Celia Cruz") and [Tito Puente](/wiki/Tito_Puente "Tito Puente"), though it was the hip hop song "[Rapper's Delight](/wiki/Rapper%27s_Delight "Rapper's Delight")" (1979) by [The Sugarhill Gang](/wiki/The_Sugarhill_Gang "The Sugarhill Gang") that she said changed her life.[[384]](#cite_note-387)

Lopez has cited [Madonna](/wiki/Madonna "Madonna") as her "first big musical influence", explaining "It was all about Madonna for me. She inspired me to want to sing, to dance, to work hard."[[385]](#cite_note-388)[[386]](#cite_note-389) Her other major influences include [Tina Turner](/wiki/Tina_Turner "Tina Turner"), [James Brown](/wiki/James_Brown "James Brown"), and [Michael Jackson](/wiki/Michael_Jackson "Michael Jackson").[[387]](#cite_note-10_Questions_for_Lopez-390) Lopez has cited [Janet Jackson](/wiki/Janet_Jackson "Janet Jackson") as a major inspiration for her own dance and music videos, stating that she "probably started dancing" because of Jackson's music video for "[The Pleasure Principle](/wiki/The_Pleasure_Principle_(song)#Music_video "The Pleasure Principle (song)")".[[388]](#cite_note-timesofindia-391)[[389]](#cite_note-392) She also looked to the careers of [Cher](/wiki/Cher "Cher"), [Bette Middler](/wiki/Bette_Middler "Bette Middler") and [Diana Ross](/wiki/Diana_Ross "Diana Ross"),[[69]](#cite_note-Parish2-71) and has been inspired by younger artists such as [Lady Gaga](/wiki/Lady_Gaga "Lady Gaga").[[390]](#cite_note-393)

### Musical style

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Gnome-mime-sound-openclipart.svg/60px-Gnome-mime-sound-openclipart.svg.png)

["Waiting for Tonight"](/wiki/File:Waiting_for_Tonight.ogg "File:Waiting for Tonight.ogg")

A sample of "[Waiting for Tonight](/wiki/Waiting_for_Tonight "Waiting for Tonight")", a [Euro](/wiki/Europop "Europop")-centric [dance-pop](/wiki/Dance-pop "Dance-pop") song with distinctly [Latin](/wiki/Latin_American_music "Latin American music") influences, which Lopez is known for incorporating in her music. It was credited with establishing her as a leading artist in the dance-pop genre.[[120]](#cite_note-Variety-122)[[391]](#cite_note-Malone-394)[[392]](#cite_note-Lockett-395)

---

*Problems playing this file? See [media help](/wiki/Help:Media "Help:Media").*

Musically, Lopez is described as a "chameleon" by outlets like *[Variety](/wiki/Variety_(magazine) "Variety (magazine)")* and *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")*, having been known to experiment and jump between genres.[[120]](#cite_note-Variety-122)[[391]](#cite_note-Malone-394)[[184]](#cite_note-Benjamin-186)[[393]](#cite_note-Statesman-396) Her music strongly identifies with hip hop while exploring the "romantic innocence" of Latin music.[[394]](#cite_note-397) *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")* observed that she has "never been one to downplay her Latinidad, and always makes sure to display it" in her music, "regardless of genre".[[391]](#cite_note-Malone-394) *On the 6* and *J.Lo*, described by Lopez as [Latin soul](/wiki/Latin_soul "Latin soul"), fuse a wide range of Latin genres with [R&B](/wiki/Contemporary_R%26B "Contemporary R&B") and hip hop, while also containing [dance-pop](/wiki/Dance-pop "Dance-pop").[[391]](#cite_note-Malone-394)[[92]](#cite_note-Woog_55-94)[[30]](#cite_note-LA_Times_99-32) While mostly English, the albums include some Spanish and bilingual songs such as "[Cariño](/wiki/Cari%C3%B1o_(song) "Cariño (song)")",[[395]](#cite_note-398) with Lopez also speaking in Spanish and asserting her Latin identity on songs like "[Let's Get Loud](/wiki/Let%27s_Get_Loud "Let's Get Loud")".[[396]](#cite_note-399)[[397]](#cite_note-400) A departure from her previous work, *This Is Me... Then* blends 1970s [soul](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music") with "streetwise" hip hop.[[97]](#cite_note-Paoletta-99) *Rebirth* was noted for its "relaxing" atmosphere, while *Como Ama una Mujer* was characterized as "silkily sedate Spanish [Sade](/wiki/Sade_(singer) "Sade (singer)") worship".[[120]](#cite_note-Variety-122) *Brave* and *Love?* delved further into dance-pop,[[134]](#cite_note-Bernstein-136)[[398]](#cite_note-Drew-401) while *A.K.A.* and *This Is Me... Now* saw her return to her R&B roots, though the latter eschews Latin influences.[[399]](#cite_note-Papi-402)[[400]](#cite_note-Daily_Telegraph_Review-403)

Described as autobiographical,[[401]](#cite_note-404) most of Lopez's music has centered around the "ups and downs" of love, often inspired by her own love life.[[402]](#cite_note-LoveMTV-405)[[403]](#cite_note-Brown-406) Lyrically, *This Is Me... Then* is largely focused on her relationship with [Ben Affleck](/wiki/Ben_Affleck "Ben Affleck"); the song "Dear Ben" was described as the album's "glowing centerpiece".[[404]](#cite_note-407) Its sequel album, *This Is Me... Now*, was informed by her marriage to Affleck, and Lopez's "psychological journey of the past two decade".[[405]](#cite_note-ThisIsMeNow_Variety-408) *Como Ama una Mujer* features introspective lyrics about romance, heartache and self-loathing.[[130]](#cite_note-CAUM-132) Regarding *Love?*, Lopez stated: "There's still so much to learn and that's why the question mark."[[402]](#cite_note-LoveMTV-405) Other recurrent themes in Lopez's music have included her upbringing in the Bronx,[[30]](#cite_note-LA_Times_99-32)[[406]](#cite_note-409) [social class](/wiki/Social_class "Social class")[[393]](#cite_note-Statesman-396) and [women's empowerment](/wiki/Women%27s_empowerment "Women's empowerment").[[407]](#cite_note-410)

Lopez has received mixed reviews for her "light" [soubrette](/wiki/Soubrette "Soubrette") voice,[[408]](#cite_note-Philly-411) which critics have described as thin,[[400]](#cite_note-Daily_Telegraph_Review-403) and overshadowed by her music's production.[[409]](#cite_note-vocall-412)[[410]](#cite_note-413)[[411]](#cite_note-Erlewine-414) With *On the 6*, critics described Lopez's voice as a "sultry purr" and "breathy";[[412]](#cite_note-NME-415)[[413]](#cite_note-416) [Rob Sheffield](/wiki/Rob_Sheffield "Rob Sheffield") remarked: "Lopez sticks to the understated R&B murmur of a round-the-way superstar who doesn't need to belt because she knows you're already paying attention ... She makes a little va-va and a whole lot of voom go a long way."[[414]](#cite_note-Rolling-417) Lopez, who often worried about her singing "not being as strong" as her other attributes, would later credit ex-husband [Marc Anthony](/wiki/Marc_Anthony "Marc Anthony") for helping her find her "best singing voice".[[415]](#cite_note-418) *[Pitchfork](/wiki/Pitchfork_(website) "Pitchfork (website)")* described her voice as being "stronger than ever" on *This Is Me... Now*, while opining that "the thinness of the voice has often been beside the point, if not a boon to her early career", which was marked by "Bronx regularness".[[128]](#cite_note-Pitchfork-130)

Lopez has been criticized for her use of background vocals from [demo](/wiki/Demo_(music) "Demo (music)") singers, prompting rumors of [ghost singers](/wiki/Playback_singer "Playback singer")' vocals being used in place of her own.[[416]](#cite_note-419)[[417]](#cite_note-420) The rumors have been debunked by writers and background singers who worked with Lopez,[[418]](#cite_note-421)[[419]](#cite_note-Milian-422) including [Christina Milian](/wiki/Christina_Milian "Christina Milian") ("[Play](/wiki/Play_(Jennifer_Lopez_song) "Play (Jennifer Lopez song)")"), who called the controversy "funny" and said: "It's no different than Michael Jackson having background singers on songs or [Britney Spears](/wiki/Britney_Spears "Britney Spears"). This is what music is made of."[[419]](#cite_note-Milian-422)

### Dance, videos and stage

See also: [Jennifer Lopez videography](/wiki/Jennifer_Lopez_videography "Jennifer Lopez videography")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/The_opening_ceremony_of_the_FIFA_World_Cup_2014_13_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-The_opening_ceremony_of_the_FIFA_World_Cup_2014_13_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_opening_ceremony_of_the_FIFA_World_Cup_2014_13_(cropped).jpg)

Lopez performing during the [2014 FIFA World Cup opening ceremony](/wiki/2014_FIFA_World_Cup_opening_ceremony "2014 FIFA World Cup opening ceremony") in [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil")

Considered one of dance's "greatest success stories",[[180]](#cite_note-Variety_July_2016-182) Lopez has received praise for her highly choreographed dancing,[[408]](#cite_note-Philly-411)[[420]](#cite_note-423)[[421]](#cite_note-424) and is noted for her technical skill.[[422]](#cite_note-425) She has been trained in a range of dance styles, including ballet, jazz, flamenco, ballroom,[[423]](#cite_note-Shall_We_Dance?_-_Interview-426) pole dancing,[[424]](#cite_note-Elena-427) breakdancing, street dance, and hip hop.[[425]](#cite_note-428) Her signature movements include "clock-wise pivoting with salsa hip circles and sequential torso undulations".[[426]](#cite_note-429) Described by [MTV](/wiki/MTV "MTV")'s Madeline Roth as "some of the most memorable visuals of the 21st century",[[427]](#cite_note-430) Lopez's music videos made her a dominant figure on MTV,[[428]](#cite_note-Asia_Pacific-431)[[429]](#cite_note-432) and helped influence [2000s fashion](/wiki/2000s_fashion "2000s fashion") trends.[[430]](#cite_note-Fader-433)[[431]](#cite_note-434) Her music videos are known for showcasing her ability as a dancer,[[432]](#cite_note-Spanos-435) which [CNN](/wiki/CNN "CNN")'s Holly Thomas said gave her a "captivating, commanding presence",[[433]](#cite_note-436) as well as themes of surveillance and fame.[[432]](#cite_note-Spanos-435)[[434]](#cite_note-Sonia-437) Her videography and dancing have also been criticized for their provocative nature,[[435]](#cite_note-IBT-438)[[436]](#cite_note-439) with Lopez being described as one of the Latin performers whose work "racialized and sexualized their bodies".[[437]](#cite_note-440)

On stage, Lopez is recognized for her showmanship, sex appeal, and glamorous costumes, which often include [bodysuits](/wiki/Bodysuit "Bodysuit").[[438]](#cite_note-LA_Times_Vegas-441)[[439]](#cite_note-442) Journalists have described her as the "ultimate [showgirl](/wiki/Showgirl "Showgirl")".[[440]](#cite_note-443)[[441]](#cite_note-444) While being noted to lip sync in the early stages of career,[[442]](#cite_note-445)[[443]](#cite_note-446) Lopez's Dance Again World Tour was praised for showcasing live vocals and choreography synchronously.[[408]](#cite_note-Philly-411)[[444]](#cite_note-447)[[445]](#cite_note-filipino-448) In a review of her Las Vegas residency All I Have, *Los Angeles Times* writer Nolan Feeney remarked that her dancing is "undoubtedly the centerpiece of the show".[[438]](#cite_note-LA_Times_Vegas-441)

Her provocative stage performances and costumes have also drawn scrutiny. [Robin Givhan](/wiki/Robin_Givhan "Robin Givhan") of *[The Daily Beast](/wiki/The_Daily_Beast "The Daily Beast")* criticized the nude bodysuit Lopez wore during her performance at the [2011 American Music Awards](/wiki/2011_American_Music_Awards "2011 American Music Awards"), calling it "banal [exhibitionism](/wiki/Exhibitionism "Exhibitionism")" which "cried out for attention in all the wrong ways".[[446]](#cite_note-449) A number of her performances have been deemed inappropriate for television,[[447]](#cite_note-450)[[448]](#cite_note-451) including her controversial appearance at the Moroccan musical festival, [Mawazine](/wiki/Mawazine "Mawazine"), in 2015. Moroccan Prime Minister [Abdelilah Benkirane](/wiki/Abdelilah_Benkirane "Abdelilah Benkirane") called it "disgraceful" and demanded that legal measures be taken, while an education group claimed that she "disturbed public order and tarnished women's honor and respect".[[449]](#cite_note-452)

### Acting and screen

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/TIFF_2019_jlo_%281_of_1%29-2_%2848696671561%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-TIFF_2019_jlo_%281_of_1%29-2_%2848696671561%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:TIFF_2019_jlo_(1_of_1)-2_(48696671561)_(cropped).jpg)

Lopez at the [Toronto Film Festival](/wiki/Toronto_Film_Festival "Toronto Film Festival") in 2019

Lopez's films often see her playing "relatable everywomen",[[450]](#cite_note-453) with Cady Lang of *[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")* writing: "A longtime hallmark of Lopez's filmography is her penchant for stories about survivors. Her characters are never shrinking violets."[[451]](#cite_note-454) Discussing the roles she is drawn to, Lopez said in 1998: "I don't think of them as strong women ... I like characters that are really part of the story as opposed to window dressing, but I think the interesting thing is that they are real people. Nobody walks around being strong all the time."[[452]](#cite_note-455) Like her music, a number of her film roles explore [class consciousness](/wiki/Class_consciousness "Class consciousness"), including *Maid in Manhattan* (2002) and *Second Act* (2018).[[453]](#cite_note-Wilkinson-456)

Film critic [Stephanie Zacharek](/wiki/Stephanie_Zacharek "Stephanie Zacharek") described Lopez as "one of the most gifted and appealing performers of the past two decades ... and yet the movies have rarely known what to do with her and her significant gifts as an actor."[[454]](#cite_note-Patron_Saint-457) While Lopez received acclaim for her early film work, *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* writer Kyle Buchanan noted that her tendency to star in romantic comedies, and her pop music career, "lowered her critical bona fides".[[60]](#cite_note-Kyle-62) [Taylor Hackford](/wiki/Taylor_Hackford "Taylor Hackford"), who directed Lopez in *Parker*, said she "can really act": "She knows how to transmit nuances, to make the subtlest of shifts. Does she have humor? Yes. But at the same time she can get into the deepest dramatic areas."[[118]](#cite_note-Van_Meter-120) Lopez, who identifies the romantic comedy as her favorite film genre,[[455]](#cite_note-458) has been referred to as the "Patron Saint of Romantic Comedies"[[454]](#cite_note-Patron_Saint-457) and the "Queen of Romantic Comedies".[[456]](#cite_note-459)[[457]](#cite_note-460)

In her early films, Lopez played stereotypical roles which signified her as a "racialized, exoticized" [other](/wiki/Other_(philosophy) "Other (philosophy)"). In films such as *Blood and Wine* and *U Turn*, her body is fetishized "through extreme close-ups" and "framed as animalistic, primitive, and irresistibly dangerous to the Anglo American male characters."[[458]](#cite_note-Latina_Iconicity-461) This shifted with her role in *Selena*, which "affirmed her Latinx identity, and won her the loyalty of that significant section of the U.S. and global audience."[[203]](#cite_note-BBC-205) She has also been criticized for playing ethnically ambiguous parts and tapping into "the ability to perform a [panethnic](/wiki/Panethnic "Panethnic") other" to commodify herself.[[458]](#cite_note-Latina_Iconicity-461) Her role as an Italian woman in *The Wedding Planner* has been described as "a case of classic Hollywood whitewashing."[[82]](#cite_note-Vice-84)

## Public image

Lopez has been a polarizing figure in popular culture.[[459]](#cite_note-Idol1-462)[[460]](#cite_note-TWP-463) Andrew Barker of *[Variety](/wiki/Variety_(magazine) "Variety (magazine)")* writes that she "exhibits a plethora of contradictions", observing: "Despite a carefully cultivated image as an imperious pop empress in ludicrously expensive outfits, her signature hits bear the titles 'I'm Real' and 'Jenny From the Block'."[[120]](#cite_note-Variety-122) In 2002, Lynette Holloway of *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* described Lopez's image as including "a dash of *ghetto fabulousness*" and "middle-class respectability" for mass appeal.[[99]](#cite_note-Lynette-101) The media discerned a shift in her public image upon joining *American Idol* in 2011,[[459]](#cite_note-Idol1-462)[[461]](#cite_note-J.Lo_2.0-464) with *[Entertainment Weekly](/wiki/Entertainment_Weekly "Entertainment Weekly")* writing: "Gone was her old cut-a-bitch swagger; J. Lo 2.0 is an all-embracing, [Oprahfied](/wiki/Oprah "Oprah") earth *[madre](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/madre "wikt:madre")*."[[462]](#cite_note-465) *[People](/wiki/People_(magazine) "People (magazine)")* editor [Peter Castro](/wiki/Peter_Castro "Peter Castro") said *American Idol* made Lopez "a celebrity of the people" whereas before, "there was a huge distance with the American public."[[461]](#cite_note-J.Lo_2.0-464)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Jennifer_Lopez_-_Pop_Music_Festival_-_23.06.2012_%287444363106%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Jennifer_Lopez_-_Pop_Music_Festival_-_23.06.2012_%287444363106%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jennifer_Lopez_-_Pop_Music_Festival_-_23.06.2012_(7444363106)_(cropped).jpg)

Lopez performing during her [Dance Again World Tour](/wiki/Dance_Again_World_Tour "Dance Again World Tour") in 2012

Lopez is widely celebrated for her curvaceous hourglass figure,[[463]](#cite_note-466)[[464]](#cite_note-467) which earned her the nickname *La Guitarra* (the guitar).[[84]](#cite_note-Divas-86) *[Vanity Fair](/wiki/Vanity_Fair_(magazine) "Vanity Fair (magazine)")* described her buttocks as "in and of themselves, a cultural icon".[[465]](#cite_note-468) Recounting her experience on early films, Lopez stated: "I've always had costume people looking at me a little weary and immediately fitting me out with things to hide my bottom."[[466]](#cite_note-469) She often wears revealing outfits to accentuate her curves.[[467]](#cite_note-470) Author Mary Beltrán opined that for Lopez to "unashamedly display her well-endowed posterior during this time period" could be viewed as "a revolutionary act with respect to Anglo beauty ideals".[[468]](#cite_note-Beltran-471) However, she was also criticized for perceived changes in her image upon launching her music career,[[468]](#cite_note-Beltran-471) which included "weight loss and lightening her hair".[[469]](#cite_note-Baez-472)

Considered a fashion icon, Lopez is known for her sense of style and "breaking fashion rules".[[470]](#cite_note-CFDA-473)[[84]](#cite_note-Divas-86)[[471]](#cite_note-474) She is recognized for both her "bold" red carpet fashion,[[472]](#cite_note-Brookins-475) including fourteen appearances at the [haute couture](/wiki/Haute_couture "Haute couture") [Met Gala](/wiki/Met_Gala "Met Gala") in New York City, and her everyday street style, which is "discussed ad infinitum by the fashion press."[[473]](#cite_note-Athleisure-476)[[474]](#cite_note-Vogue_CFDA-477) *[British Vogue](/wiki/British_Vogue "British Vogue")* said she "added megawatt glamour to every outfit she wore",[[272]](#cite_note-StyleRules-274) with *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")* describing her style as "scantily clad".[[475]](#cite_note-Scla-478) Lopez's style has been influenced by "women who epitomize old Hollywood glamour like [Rita Hayworth](/wiki/Rita_Hayworth "Rita Hayworth")", as well as her Bronx upbringing and [Nuyorican](/wiki/Nuyorican "Nuyorican") identity.[[476]](#cite_note-Chambers-479) Described as the "ultimate [sex symbol](/wiki/Sex_symbol "Sex symbol")" of the early 21st century,[[477]](#cite_note-Greatest_Pop_Star-480) various publications have ranked her as the most beautiful or sexiest woman in the world, including *People*,[[144]](#cite_note-Forbes2-146) *[People en Español](/wiki/People_en_Espa%C3%B1ol "People en Español")*,[[478]](#cite_note-481) *[Vibe](/wiki/Vibe_(magazine) "Vibe (magazine)")*,[[479]](#cite_note-482) *[Details](/wiki/Details_(magazine) "Details (magazine)")*,[[480]](#cite_note-483) and *[FHM](/wiki/FHM "FHM")*, with Lopez topping the latter's [100 Sexiest Women](/wiki/FHM%27s_100_Sexiest_Women_(UK) "FHM's 100 Sexiest Women (UK)") list twice.[[481]](#cite_note-484) Lopez has criticized "this funny notion in America that you can't be a mom and be sexy", stating: "The truth is that women can be sexy until the day they die."[[163]](#cite_note-HuffPost_Latino-165)

She has a large following on social media, being the [14th most-followed individual](/wiki/List_of_most-followed_Instagram_accounts "List of most-followed Instagram accounts") on Instagram and having one of the [50 most-followed accounts](/wiki/List_of_most-followed_Twitter_accounts "List of most-followed Twitter accounts") on Twitter.[[482]](#cite_note-485)[[483]](#cite_note-486) On the [Recording Industry Association of America](/wiki/Recording_Industry_Association_of_America "Recording Industry Association of America") (RIAA)'s "Music Fuels" ranking, Lopez is the eighth-most-followed musician across Instagram, Twitter, and Facebook.[[484]](#cite_note-487) She is the richest Latin actress, with an estimated net worth of US$400 million.[[485]](#cite_note-488)[[486]](#cite_note-489)

Described as "one of the most photographed women in the world",[[487]](#cite_note-490) Lopez has long been a tabloid fixture, and admits to having a "less-than-perfect" public image.[[488]](#cite_note-491)[[489]](#cite_note-492) [Frances Negrón-Muntaner](/wiki/Frances_Negr%C3%B3n-Muntaner "Frances Negrón-Muntaner") wrote that by 1999, "Internet chat rooms exploded with anti-Lopez babble" and she became "one of the easiest moving targets for cheap laughs as well as anxieties about working-class 'loud' sexuality and specifically Latino visibility."[[490]](#cite_note-493) Much of the media coverage surrounding Lopez in the 2000s "focused on her dramatic private life",[[69]](#cite_note-Parish2-71) with several writers describing her as overexposed at the time, despite her continued success.[[99]](#cite_note-Lynette-101)[[491]](#cite_note-494) Media outlets often liken her to actress [Elizabeth Taylor](/wiki/Elizabeth_Taylor "Elizabeth Taylor"), due to her numerous failed relationships and glamorous public persona.[[492]](#cite_note-495)[[493]](#cite_note-W2013-496)[[494]](#cite_note-497) Her high-profile relationship with Combs was described as having "defined an age",[[106]](#cite_note-Observer-108) with *Vanity Fair* writing that they entered "the pantheon of slightly notorious celebrity couples: [Liz](/wiki/Elizabeth_Taylor "Elizabeth Taylor") and [Dick](/wiki/Richard_Burton "Richard Burton"), Frank and Ava."[[495]](#cite_note-Zeman-498)

Throughout her career, she has also received a bad reputation as being a demanding and outspoken "diva",[[496]](#cite_note-499)[[468]](#cite_note-Beltran-471) something which she denies, stating: "I've always been fascinated by how much more well-behaved we have to be than men."[[497]](#cite_note-500) Some of the backlash and fabricated tabloid stories surrounding Lopez have been attributed to her 1998 interview with *[Movieline](/wiki/Movieline "Movieline")*, in which she was quoted as criticizing various actors and directors. Lopez, who "cried for hours" after the article was published, said the comments were made in jest and she was "so misquoted and so taken out of context".[[495]](#cite_note-Zeman-498) In 2003, [Lawrence Donegan](/wiki/Lawrence_Donegan "Lawrence Donegan") of *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* posited that "indefensible" misogyny and racism were to blame for her position as "the most vilified woman in modern popular culture".[[106]](#cite_note-Observer-108) In 2021, [Ben Affleck](/wiki/Ben_Affleck "Ben Affleck") said: "People were so fucking mean about her; sexist, racist, ugly vicious shit was written about her in ways that if you wrote it now, you would literally be fired for saying some of the things you said."[[498]](#cite_note-501)

## Legacy and cultural impact

Main article: [Cultural impact of Jennifer Lopez](/wiki/Cultural_impact_of_Jennifer_Lopez "Cultural impact of Jennifer Lopez")

### Entertainment industry

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Jennifer_Lopez_%28Hollywood_Walk_of_Fame_stars%29.jpg/250px-Jennifer_Lopez_%28Hollywood_Walk_of_Fame_stars%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jennifer_Lopez_(Hollywood_Walk_of_Fame_stars).jpg)

Lopez's star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame

Regarded as one of the most influential performers of her time,[[499]](#cite_note-502) Lopez is often cited by journalists and academics as the most influential entertainer of Latin descent,[[500]](#cite_note-503)[[501]](#cite_note-Run_the_World-504) credited with breaking racial barriers in the entertainment industry.[[502]](#cite_note-Parish_5-505)[[503]](#cite_note-Forbes3-506) She was described as "the first Latina superstar" by writer [Ned Zeman](/wiki/Ned_Zeman "Ned Zeman"),[[495]](#cite_note-Zeman-498) and the "embodiment of the [American Dream](/wiki/American_Dream "American Dream")" by Robert C. Cottrell in *Icons of American Popular Culture* (2009).[[504]](#cite_note-507) [Maer Roshan](/wiki/Maer_Roshan "Maer Roshan"), editor-in-chief of *[The Hollywood Reporter](/wiki/The_Hollywood_Reporter "The Hollywood Reporter")*, said "Lopez has broken barriers and redefined what’s possible for women and Latinos in entertainment."[[505]](#cite_note-508) Often dubbed the "[Queen of Dance](/wiki/Honorific_nicknames_in_popular_music "Honorific nicknames in popular music")" by media outlets,[[506]](#cite_note-509) she is considered a [triple threat](/wiki/Triple_threat_(entertainer) "Triple threat (entertainer)") performer,[[203]](#cite_note-BBC-205)[[507]](#cite_note-510)[[508]](#cite_note-511) and was one of few actors to successfully cross over into music.[[509]](#cite_note-512) [Rachel Sklar](/wiki/Rachel_Sklar "Rachel Sklar") of *[The Huffington Post](/wiki/The_Huffington_Post "The Huffington Post")* called Lopez a trailblazer, writing: "[Beyoncé](/wiki/Beyonc%C3%A9 "Beyoncé"), [Miley](/wiki/Miley "Miley"), Gaga, any actress who's ever released an album — they are all standing on the shoulders of J.Lo. She was a true triple threat — actress, singer, dancer ... and packaged it sometimes outrageously but always sexily."[[510]](#cite_note-Sklar-513) Lopez is considered a [pop icon](/wiki/Pop_icon "Pop icon"),[[511]](#cite_note-Cottrell1-514)[[512]](#cite_note-515) with [VH1](/wiki/VH1 "VH1") ranking her at number 15 on their list of [200 Greatest Pop Culture Icons](/wiki/The_Greatest_(VH1_TV_series) "The Greatest (VH1 TV series)"),[[513]](#cite_note-516) number 16 on 100 Greatest Women In Music,[[514]](#cite_note-517) and number 21 on 50 Greatest Women of the Video Era.[[515]](#cite_note-518)

The highest-paid Latina actress in history,[[516]](#cite_note-519)[[517]](#cite_note-520) Lopez's film career has been credited with paving the way for greater representation of [Latino Americans](/wiki/Latino_Americans "Latino Americans") in Hollywood, a historically white space.[[518]](#cite_note-521)[[519]](#cite_note-therecord-522)[[468]](#cite_note-Beltran-471)[[520]](#cite_note-Oprah_Daily-523) Biographer Kathleen Tracy opined that "Lopez blazed a trail no openly Latin actress had gone before", noting that previous Latina stars cosmetically downplayed their ethnicity and changed their names.[[521]](#cite_note-524) Lopez, who achieved "ethnic 'blind' casting" with *Out of Sight* (1998),[[522]](#cite_note-Negrón-Muntaner,_244-245-525) is noted by biographer [James Robert Parish](/wiki/James_Robert_Parish "James Robert Parish") to have "helped minimize racial boundaries in show business" by deliberately avoiding stereotypical roles.[[502]](#cite_note-Parish_5-505)

With her musical debut, Lopez helped influence the Latin pop movement in American music, often dubbed the "[Latin explosion](/wiki/Latin_American_music_in_the_United_States#1990s_Latin_explosion "Latin American music in the United States")" of the 1990s.[[501]](#cite_note-Run_the_World-504)[[523]](#cite_note-526) [The Recording Academy](/wiki/The_Recording_Academy "The Recording Academy") called her the "breakout female star" of the Latin pop movement,[[524]](#cite_note-Recording_Academy_2017-527) with historian [Roger Bruns](/wiki/Roger_Bruns "Roger Bruns") describing her as "an influential force in driving a growing Latin cultural influence in popular music" at the time.[[65]](#cite_note-Bruns_299-67) Ella Cerón of *[Teen Vogue](/wiki/Teen_Vogue "Teen Vogue")* wrote: "In an era of blonde, [bubblegum pop](/wiki/Bubblegum_pop "Bubblegum pop"), there was only one J.Lo."[[525]](#cite_note-528) She has been credited with paving the way for U.S.-born Latina artists including [Selena Gomez](/wiki/Selena_Gomez "Selena Gomez"), [Becky G](/wiki/Becky_G "Becky G"), and [Cardi B](/wiki/Cardi_B "Cardi B"),[[526]](#cite_note-529)[[520]](#cite_note-Oprah_Daily-523) and for Latin American artists to venture into English pop music, including [Shakira](/wiki/Shakira "Shakira"), [Thalía](/wiki/Thal%C3%ADa "Thalía") and [Paulina Rubio](/wiki/Paulina_Rubio "Paulina Rubio").[[527]](#cite_note-Aida-530) Along with [Ricky Martin](/wiki/Ricky_Martin "Ricky Martin"), she helped increase the visibility of Latin music in international markets including East Asia and Canada.[[428]](#cite_note-Asia_Pacific-431)[[528]](#cite_note-531) Due to her "multi-tasking career", she has been described as "the next-generation [Madonna](/wiki/Madonna "Madonna")",[[529]](#cite_note-Chicago_Tribune_2002-532) and "the first woman to demonstrate that a Madonna could be a Latina".[[393]](#cite_note-Statesman-396)

Lopez's music has been credited with helping influence the trajectory of [2000s](/wiki/2000s_in_music "2000s in music") music,[[71]](#cite_note-Shemesh-73) including dance music,[[120]](#cite_note-Variety-122)[[392]](#cite_note-Lockett-395) Latin music,[[530]](#cite_note-533) R&B,[[531]](#cite_note-534) and [pop rap](/wiki/Pop_rap "Pop rap") collaborations.[[87]](#cite_note-I'm_Real_ET-89)[[71]](#cite_note-Shemesh-73) *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")* magazine described her as having "helped define an era where hip-hop, R&B and pop all got more cuddly together than ever before".[[532]](#cite_note-greatestpop21stcentury-535) Scholar [Miriam Jiménez Román](/wiki/Miriam_Jim%C3%A9nez_Rom%C3%A1n "Miriam Jiménez Román") suggested that Lopez, who represents the "often-suppressed" history of Puerto Ricans in hip hop culture, was "able to traverse the difficult racial boundaries".[[533]](#cite_note-536) In July 2023, [Madame Tussauds New York](/wiki/Madame_Tussauds_New_York "Madame Tussauds New York") unveiled a wax figure of Lopez inspired by her performance at the [Super Bowl LIV halftime show](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LIV "Super Bowl LIV").[[534]](#cite_note-537)

### Cultural influence

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Jennifer_Lopez_with_Flag.jpg/250px-Jennifer_Lopez_with_Flag.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jennifer_Lopez_with_Flag.jpg)

Lopez waving the [Puerto Rican flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Puerto_Rico "Flag of Puerto Rico") in 2009 at the [Puerto Rican Day Parade](/wiki/Puerto_Rican_Day_Parade "Puerto Rican Day Parade") in [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan")

In 2012, *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")* ranked Lopez as the [world's most powerful celebrity](/wiki/Celebrity_100 "Celebrity 100") and the [38th most powerful woman](/wiki/Forbes_list_of_the_World%27s_100_Most_Powerful_Women "Forbes list of the World's 100 Most Powerful Women").[[144]](#cite_note-Forbes2-146)[[535]](#cite_note-538) She was named one of *[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")*'s [100 most influential people in the world](/wiki/Time_100 "Time 100") in 2018.[[536]](#cite_note-Time_100-539) Lopez's influence has been attributed to her bicultural identity and ability to [code-switch](/wiki/Code-switch "Code-switch").[[537]](#cite_note-540) According to *[Latina](/wiki/Latina_(magazine) "Latina (magazine)")* magazine founder [Christy Haubegger](/wiki/Christy_Haubegger "Christy Haubegger"), Lopez was "the first icon that generationally fits" young Latino Americans who followed [celebrity culture](/wiki/Celebrity_culture "Celebrity culture").[[460]](#cite_note-TWP-463) Lopez was featured on the first cover of *Latina* in 1996, with editor Galina Espinoza writing in 2011 that there is "no recounting of modern Latina history without Jennifer".[[538]](#cite_note-541) She has been credited with empowering Puerto Ricans, a group "which has been subdued to different forms of discrimination" since Puerto Rico was [annexed by the U.S.](/wiki/Puerto_Rican_campaign "Puerto Rican campaign")[[539]](#cite_note-542)

Jonathan Van Meter of *[Vogue](/wiki/Vogue_(magazine) "Vogue (magazine)")* described Lopez as having "changed the face of modern celebrity."[[118]](#cite_note-Van_Meter-120) Her ability to start trends has prompted the usage of the phrase "the J.Lo effect" in popular culture, which has been used in various different contexts,[[540]](#cite_note-Allure-543)[[541]](#cite_note-544)[[542]](#cite_note-Bahr-545) including the trend of networks hiring "big name" judges for reality competition programs after Lopez's successful casting on *American Idol*.[[543]](#cite_note-546) With her moniker J.Lo, Lopez started a trend of celebrities being given abbreviated nicknames, while "[Bennifer](/wiki/Bennifer "Bennifer")" began the convention of celebrity couple [name blending](/wiki/Name_blending "Name blending").[[393]](#cite_note-Statesman-396)[[544]](#cite_note-547)

Described by scholar Helene A. Shugart of the [University of Utah](/wiki/University_of_Utah "University of Utah") as "arguably the most visible Latina in contemporary mainstream popular culture", Lopez has been the subject of considerable [academic analysis](/wiki/Academic_writing "Academic writing"), including in relation to ethnicity, class, "body politics",[[545]](#cite_note-548) race, Latin culture, and gender.[[546]](#cite_note-549)[[469]](#cite_note-Baez-472) While credited with opening "the door to [Latino culture having a] broader appeal",[[203]](#cite_note-BBC-205) Lopez has also been a polarizing figure, generating "often vehement disagreement about what is and isn't Latino."[[460]](#cite_note-TWP-463) According to journalist Jesús Triviño Alarcón, Lopez "widened the scope of what it means to be Latina by simply being her. You don’t have to speak perfect Spanish, or Spanish at all, to be proud of your roots."[[520]](#cite_note-Oprah_Daily-523)

As a result of the [public emphasis on](#Public_image) her curvaceous figure, Lopez has been credited with influencing a shift in cultural [beauty standards](/wiki/Feminine_beauty_ideal "Feminine beauty ideal").[[393]](#cite_note-Statesman-396)[[547]](#cite_note-550)[[548]](#cite_note-551)[[549]](#cite_note-552) The surge in popularity of [buttock augmentation](/wiki/Buttock_augmentation "Buttock augmentation") surgery in the early 2000s was attributed to Lopez.[[550]](#cite_note-553)[[551]](#cite_note-554) In *Latin Sensations* (2001), Herón Marquez wrote: "Because she wasn't rail thin, Lopez had broken the mold ... Suddenly, it was okay for women to have hips, curves, and a big backside."[[552]](#cite_note-555) This has been considered a sign of Lopez's social power in "changing cultural standards within the mainstream media as a whole".[[553]](#cite_note-556)

In 2014, scientists named a species of aquatic [mite](/wiki/Mite "Mite") found in Puerto Rico, *[Litarachna lopezae](/wiki/Litarachna_lopezae "Litarachna lopezae")*, after Lopez.[[554]](#cite_note-557) In [Tok Pisin](/wiki/Tok_Pisin "Tok Pisin"), a language spoken in [Papua New Guinea](/wiki/Papua_New_Guinea "Papua New Guinea"), the term *[palopa](/wiki/Palopa "Palopa")* is used to describe non-heteronormative people whose identities may correlate with those who, in Western contexts, identify as part of the LGBTQ+ community. The term is a contraction of Jennifer Lopez, which was used as a coded expression within the community.[[555]](#cite_note-:12-558)[[556]](#cite_note-559) In 2019, she was presented with keys to the city of [Miami Beach](/wiki/Miami_Beach "Miami Beach"), where July 24 was declared "Jennifer Lopez Day".[[557]](#cite_note-560)

### Fashion and branding

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Donatella_Versace_and_Jennifer_Lopez_in_2019.jpg/250px-Donatella_Versace_and_Jennifer_Lopez_in_2019.jpg)](/wiki/File:Donatella_Versace_and_Jennifer_Lopez_in_2019.jpg)

Lopez (*left*), wearing an updated version of her iconic [green Versace dress](/wiki/Green_Versace_dress_of_Jennifer_Lopez "Green Versace dress of Jennifer Lopez"), and [Donatella Versace](/wiki/Donatella_Versace "Donatella Versace") (*right*) in 2019

The [Council of Fashion Designers of America](/wiki/Council_of_Fashion_Designers_of_America "Council of Fashion Designers of America") presented Lopez with its Fashion Icon Award in 2019 for her "long-standing and global impact on fashion".[[470]](#cite_note-CFDA-473) Her [green Versace "Jungle Dress"](/wiki/Green_Versace_dress_of_Jennifer_Lopez "Green Versace dress of Jennifer Lopez") was voted the fifth most iconic red carpet dress of all time in a poll run by *[The Daily Telegraph](/wiki/The_Daily_Telegraph "The Daily Telegraph")*.[[558]](#cite_note-Telegraph08-561) The dress had a significant impact on the fashion industry, celebrity endorsements, and the evolution of [red carpet fashion](/wiki/Red_carpet_fashion "Red carpet fashion").[[559]](#cite_note-562)[[472]](#cite_note-Brookins-475)[[560]](#cite_note-563)[[561]](#cite_note-Riley_241-564) The images of Lopez wearing the dress became the most popular search query of all time at that point, and subsequently led to the creation of [Google](/wiki/Google_Search "Google Search")'s [image search](/wiki/Image_search "Image search").[[562]](#cite_note-565) Lopez has been credited with popularizing various fashion and beauty trends throughout her career.[[563]](#cite_note-566)[[564]](#cite_note-CNN_Juicy-567)[[128]](#cite_note-Pitchfork-130)[[565]](#cite_note-Hats-568)[[566]](#cite_note-569) In 2003, she inspired a trend of curvier [mannequins](/wiki/Mannequin "Mannequin") being designed, which fuelled a rise in sales for manufacturers and retailers.[[567]](#cite_note-570)[[568]](#cite_note-571)[[569]](#cite_note-572) Lopez's personal style and brand deals have had a considerable effect on sales in the fashion industry.[[529]](#cite_note-Chicago_Tribune_2002-532)[[565]](#cite_note-Hats-568)[[570]](#cite_note-573) The success of [Louis Vuitton](/wiki/Louis_Vuitton "Louis Vuitton")'s fall 2003 campaign, for which Lopez was the brand's model, led to more Hollywood stars "becoming image models of fashion and cosmetic labels".[[571]](#cite_note-574) In 2021, *The Daily Telegraph* reported that she was among ten celebrities whose personal style choices drove the greatest spikes in searches and news coverage for brands.[[572]](#cite_note-Abraham-575) Her appearance at [Milan Fashion Week](/wiki/Milan_Fashion_Week "Milan Fashion Week") in 2019 generated over $31.8 million in total media impact value.[[573]](#cite_note-576)[[574]](#cite_note-577)

Described as a "branding pioneer", Lopez has been credited with ushering in a new era of [celebrity branding](/wiki/Celebrity_branding "Celebrity branding").[[561]](#cite_note-Riley_241-564)[[575]](#cite_note-Adweek-578)[[118]](#cite_note-Van_Meter-120) Her first fragrance, [Glow by JLo](/wiki/Glow_by_JLo "Glow by JLo"), influenced the rise of celebrity fragrances in the 2000s.[[576]](#cite_note-WWD-579)[[577]](#cite_note-580)[[561]](#cite_note-Riley_241-564)[[578]](#cite_note-581) Her fragrance line became the most successful celebrity line in the world, with sales exceeding $2 billion as of 2012[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Jennifer_Lopez&action=edit).[[579]](#cite_note-582) Lopez's [clothing brand](/wiki/J.Lo_by_Jennifer_Lopez "J.Lo by Jennifer Lopez") was one of the first celebrity fashion lines;[[580]](#cite_note-583) scholars have credited her with redefining "how fashion is produced and entwined with other artistic endeavors",[[527]](#cite_note-Aida-530) and popularizing a trend of utilizing racial ambiguity for branding purposes in fashion and cosmetics.[[581]](#cite_note-584) As of 2022, her business ventures have cumulatively earned over US$5 billion in sales globally.[[582]](#cite_note-Stats_2022-585)

## Achievements

See also: [List of awards and nominations received by Jennifer Lopez](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Jennifer_Lopez "List of awards and nominations received by Jennifer Lopez")

As of 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Jennifer_Lopez&action=edit), Lopez has sold more than 80 million records worldwide and amassed over 15 billion streams; her films have grossed a cumulative total of US$3.1 billion while her business ventures have cumulatively made over US$5 billion in consumer sales.[[582]](#cite_note-Stats_2022-585) She remains the only female entertainer to have a number-one album and film simultaneously in the United States.[[583]](#cite_note-586) The *[Guinness World Records](/wiki/Guinness_World_Records "Guinness World Records")* acknowledged *J to tha L–O! The Remixes* (2002) as the first number-one [remix album](/wiki/Remix_album "Remix album") on the U.S. [*Billboard* 200](/wiki/Billboard_200 "Billboard 200"),[[584]](#cite_note-587) and recognized "On the Floor" as the "Highest Viewed Female Music Video of All Time" in 2012.[[585]](#cite_note-588) *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")* named Lopez the top [Hot 100](/wiki/Hot_100 "Hot 100") female artist and the [Greatest Pop Star](/wiki/Billboard%27s_Greatest_Pop_Stars#By_year "Billboard's Greatest Pop Stars") of 2001.[[586]](#cite_note-589)[[477]](#cite_note-Greatest_Pop_Star-480) *Billboard* ranked her as the ninth-greatest dance club artist of all time in 2016, having scored 18 number-one songs on its [Dance Club Songs](/wiki/Dance_Club_Songs "Dance Club Songs") chart.[[587]](#cite_note-590)[[70]](#cite_note-Recording-Academy-72) The magazine also ranked her at number 22 on its "Top 100 Women Artists of the 21st Century" list (2025).[[588]](#cite_note-591) She was named the top Latin touring artist of the decade by *[Pollstar](/wiki/Pollstar "Pollstar")* in 2021.[[589]](#cite_note-592)

For her contributions to the music industry, Lopez was honored by the [World Music Awards](/wiki/World_Music_Awards "World Music Awards") with the Legend Award in 2010,[[590]](#cite_note-593) and was presented with the prestigious landmark 2,500th star on the [Hollywood Walk of Fame](/wiki/Hollywood_Walk_of_Fame "Hollywood Walk of Fame") in 2013.[[591]](#cite_note-594) At the [2014 *Billboard* Music Awards](/wiki/2014_Billboard_Music_Awards "2014 Billboard Music Awards"), she became the first female recipient of the [*Billboard* Icon Award](/wiki/Billboard_Icon_Award "Billboard Icon Award").[[592]](#cite_note-595) In 2017, she was awarded the [Telemundo](/wiki/Telemundo "Telemundo") Star Award at the [*Billboard* Latin Music Awards](/wiki/Billboard_Latin_Music_Awards "Billboard Latin Music Awards").[[593]](#cite_note-596) Lopez received the [Michael Jackson Video Vanguard Award](/wiki/Michael_Jackson_Video_Vanguard_Award "Michael Jackson Video Vanguard Award") during the [2018 MTV Video Music Awards](/wiki/2018_MTV_Video_Music_Awards "2018 MTV Video Music Awards"), making her the first Latin performer to receive the honor.[[594]](#cite_note-597) She later received the [Icon Award](/wiki/IHeartRadio_Music_Awards#iHeartRadio_Icon_Award "IHeartRadio Music Awards") at the [iHeart Radio Music Awards](/wiki/2022_iHeartRadio_Music_Awards "2022 iHeartRadio Music Awards") in 2022.[[595]](#cite_note-598) Outside of music, [Univision](/wiki/Univision "Univision") presented her with the World Icon Award in its [Premios Juventud](/wiki/2013_Premios_Juventud "2013 Premios Juventud") in 2013.[[596]](#cite_note-599) She received the [Generation Award](/wiki/MTV_Movie_%26_TV_Awards#Generation_Award "MTV Movie & TV Awards") at the [MTV Movie & TV Awards](/wiki/MTV_Movie_%26_TV_Awards "MTV Movie & TV Awards") for her contributions to film and television in 2022.[[597]](#cite_note-600)
